elements.sent-2411-001: Relativistic full weak-neutral axial-vector four-current distributions inside a general spin-$\frac{1 {2}$ system are systematically studied for the first time, where the second-class current contribution associated with the induced (pseudo-)tensor form factor (FF) is included.}
elements.sent-2411-002: For experimental measurements, we explicitly derive the first exact full tree-level unpolarized differential cross sections of both (anti)neutrino-nucleon and (anti)neutrino-antinucleon elastic scatterings.
elements.sent-2411-003: We clearly demonstrate that the 3D axial charge distribution in the Breit frame, being purely imaginary and parity-odd, is in fact related to the induced (pseudo-)tensor FF $G_T^Z(Q^2)$ rather than the axial FF $G_A^Z(Q^2)$.
elements.sent-2411-004: We study the frame-dependence of full axial-vector four-current distributions for a moving system, and compared them with their light-front counterparts.
elements.sent-2411-005: We clarify the role played by Melosh rotations, and classify the origin of distortions in light-front distributions into three key sources using the lemma that we have proposed and verified in this work.
elements.sent-2411-006: In particular, we show that the second-class current contribution, although explicitly included, does not contribute in fact to the mean-square axial and spin radii.
elements.sent-2411-007: We finally illustrate our results in the case of a proton using the weak-neutral axial-vector FFs extracted from experimental data.
elements.sent-2411-008: Nucleons (i.e. protons and neutrons) are key hadrons to study for understanding quantum chromodynamics (QCD), since they are responsible for more than $99\%$ of the visible-matter mass in the universe~\cite{Gao:2021sml .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Protons, in particular, also hold another unique role of being the only stable composite building blocks in nature~\cite{Li:2022sqg .}
elements.sent-2411-010: Due to the complicated nonperturbative dynamics of their quark and gluon degrees of freedom, nucleons inherit particularly rich and intricate internal structures.
elements.sent-2411-011: Properties and internal structures of these key QCD bound-state systems in the weak sector become increasingly important when come to the experimental studies of nucleon axial-vector form factors (FFs) in high-energy elastic or quasielastic (anti)neutrino-(anti)nucleon scatterings~\cite{Singh:1971md,Mann:1973pr,Barish:1977qk,Baker:1981su,Miller:1982qi,Kitagaki:1983px,Horstkotte:1981ne,Ahrens:1986xe,Allasia:1990uy,Kitagaki:1990vs,K2K:2006odf,MiniBooNE:2010bsu,MiniBooNE:2010xqw,CLAS:2012ich,MINERvA:2013bcy,MiniBooNE:2013dds,Meyer:2016oeg,MINERvA:2023avz,Pate:2008va,Pate:2024acz .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Axial-vector FFs are Lorentz-invariant functions that describe how the hadron reacts with the probing (anti)neutrino in a scattering reaction, encoding therefore very clean internal axial charge and spin information of the hadron in the weak sector since the (anti)neutrinos participate only in weak interactions.
elements.sent-2411-013: In the Standard Model, there are in general two types of axial-vector FFs of a hadron in the weak sector: the weak-charged ones via the weak-charged current interactions mediated by the $W^\pm$ bosons, and the weak-neutral ones via the weak-neutral current interactions mediated by the $Z^0$ bosons.
elements.sent-2411-014: These axial-vector FFs also serve as important quantities for constraining the systematic uncertainties of high-precision measurements in (anti)neutrino oscillation experiments~\cite{OPERA:2019kzo,DoubleChooz:2019qbj,T2K:2024wfn,DUNE:2024wvj,KamLAND:2022ptk,DayaBay:2024nip,IceCube:2024xjj,JUNO:2024jaw,NOvA:2024imi .}
elements.sent-2411-015: On the theory side, tremendous progress has been made in the last few years from first principle lattice QCD calculations of these axial-vector FFs~\cite{Bhattacharya:2016zcn,Liang:2016fgy,Green:2017keo,Gupta:2017dwj,Yao:2017fym,Hasan:2017wwt,Ishikawa:2018rew,Shintani:2018ozy,Hasan:2019noy,Jang:2019vkm,RQCD:2019jai,Lin:2020rxa,Alexandrou:2020okk,Park:2021ypf,Alexandrou:2021wzv,Ishikawa:2021eut,Meyer:2021vfq,Schulz:2021kwz,Alexandrou:2021jok,Lin:2021brq,Alexandrou:2022yrr,Djukanovic:2022wru,Lin:2022nnj,Koponen:2022gfe,Jang:2023zts,Alexandrou:2023qbg ; theoretical evaluations of these axial-vector FFs and cross sections based on chiral perturbation theory and various models are still vividly developing~\cite{Meissner:1986xg,Meissner:1986js,Bernard:1994wn,Ohlsson:1998bk,Barquilla-Cano:2002btm,Silva:2005fa,Schindler:2006jq,Aliev:2007qu,Sharma:2009hg,Eichmann:2011pv,Liu:2014owa,Dahiya:2014jfa,Ramalho:2015jem,Anikin:2016teg,Mamedov:2016ype,Hashamipour:2019pgy,Mondal:2019jdg,Zhang:2019iyx,Jun:2020lfx,Chen:2020wuq,Chen:2021guo,Ahmady:2021qed,Sauerwein:2021jxb,Xu:2021wwj,Atayev:2022omk,Chen:2022odn,Cheng:2022jxe,Liu:2022ekr,Ramalho:2024tdi,Tomalak:2022xup,Sobczyk:2024ecl,SajjadAthar:2024etq .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: For (recent) reviews of nucleon axial-vector FFs and associated physics of (anti)neutrino interactions, see e.g. Refs.~\cite{LlewellynSmith:1971uhs,Gourdin:1974iq,Bernard:1995dp,Bernard:2001rs,Gorringe:2002xx,Beise:2004py,Gallagher:2011zza,Formaggio:2012cpf,Morfin:2012kn,Gonzalez-Jimenez:2011qkf,Alvarez-Ruso:2014bla,Mosel:2016cwa,NuSTEC:2017hzk,Hill:2017wgb,Meyer:2022mix,SajjadAthar:2022pjt .}
elements.sent-2411-017: According to textbooks, charge distributions can be defined in the Breit frame (BF) in term of three-dimensional (3D) Fourier transform of the Sachs electric FF~\cite{Ernst:1960zza,Sachs:1962zzc,Gao:2021sml .}
elements.sent-2411-018: However, relativistic recoil corrections spoil their interpretation as probabilistic distributions~\cite{Yennie:1957rmp,Breit:1964ga,Kelly:2002if,Burkardt:2000za,Belitsky:2003nz,Jaffe:2020ebz .}
elements.sent-2411-019: In position space, a probabilistic density interpretation is tied to Galilean symmetry that implies the invariance of inertia under the change of frames.
elements.sent-2411-020: In a relativistic theory, inertia becomes however a frame-dependent concept because of Lorentz symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-021: The only way out is to switch to the light-front (LF) formalism~\cite{Brodsky:1997de where a Galilean subgroup of the Lorentz group is singled out~\cite{Susskind:1967rg,Kogut:1969xa , allowing therefore a nice probabilistic interpretation~\cite{Burkardt:2002hr,Miller:2007uy,Carlson:2007xd,Alexandrou:2008bn,Alexandrou:2009hs,Carlson:2009ovh,Gorchtein:2009qq,Miller:2010nz,Miller:2018ybm,Freese:2023jcp,Freese:2023abr .}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: The price to pay is that besides losing the longitudinal spatial dimension\footnote{We note that Miller and Brodsky~\cite{Miller:2019ysh have recently demonstrated at the wavefunction level that frame-independent and three-dimensional LF coordinate-space wavefunctions can be obtained by using the dimensionless, frame-independent longitudinal coordinate $\tilde z$.}}
elements.sent-2411-023: The quantum phase-space formalism distinguishes itself by the fact that the requirement of a strict probabilistic interpretation is relaxed and replaced by a milder quasiprobabilistic picture~\cite{Wigner:1932eb,Hillery:1983ms,Bialynicki-Birula:1991jwl .}
elements.sent-2411-024: This approach is quite appealing since it allows one to define in a consistent way relativistic spatial distributions inside a target with arbitrary spin and arbitrary average momentum~\cite{Lorce:2017wkb,Lorce:2018zpf,Lorce:2018egm,Lorce:2020onh,Lorce:2021gxs,Lorce:2022jyi,Lorce:2022cle,Chen:2022smg,Chen:2023dxp,Chen:2024oxx,Hong:2023tkv , providing a smooth and neutral connection between the BF and essentially the LF pictures\footnote{Strictly speaking, the smooth connection is between the 2D BF and 2D infinite-momentum frame (IMF) distributions, see e.g., Refs.~\cite{Lorce:2020onh,Chen:2022smg,Chen:2023dxp,Chen:2024oxx .}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: However, IMF distributions coincide most of the time with the corresponding LF distributions~\cite{Chen:2023dxp .}
elements.sent-2411-026: As a extension of our recent work~\cite{Chen:2024oxx , we study in this work in detail the frame-dependence of the relativistic weak-neutral axial-vector four-current distributions inside a general spin-$\tfrac{1 {2}$ hadron (the nucleon for instance), where the second-class current contribution associated with the induced (pseudo-)tensor FF $G_T^Z(Q^2)$ is newly taken into account in terms of full matrix elements.}}
elements.sent-2411-027: For on-going and future experimental measurements and data analysis in elastic (anti)neutrino-(anti)nucleon scatterings~\cite{Horstkotte:1981ne,Ahrens:1986xe,MiniBooNE:2010xqw,MiniBooNE:2013dds , we also derive the first exact full tree-level unpolarized differential cross sections of both (anti)neutrino-nucleon and (anti)neutrino-antinucleon elastic scatterings in the lab frame using the full vertex functions including $6$ FFs individually.}
elements.sent-2411-028: We explicitly demonstrate that the relativistic 3D weak-neutral axial charge distribution in the BF, being purely imaginary and parity-odd, is in fact related to the weak-neutral induced (pseudo-)tensor FF $G_T^Z(Q^2)$ rather than the axial FF $G_A^Z(Q^2)$.
elements.sent-2411-029: For the derivations of LF amplitudes, we proposed and verified the following lemma: {\em Any light-front amplitudes for well-defined light-front distributions in principle can be explicitly reproduced from the corresponding elastic-frame amplitudes in the proper infinite-momentum-frame limit .}
elements.sent-2411-030: As a reward, we can classify more clearly the origin of distortions appearing in LF distributions in general into three key sources.
elements.sent-2411-031: For completeness, both 3D and 2D transverse mean-square axial and spin radii in different frames are explicitly rederived, where we show in particular that the second-class current contribution, although explicitly included in our calculations, does not contribute in fact to the mean-square axial and spin radii.
elements.sent-2411-032: The paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-033: In Sec.~\ref{sec:Elastic (anti)neutrino-(anti)nucleon scatterings , we first briefly review the matrix elements of weak-neutral axial-vector and vector four-current operators and associated FFs for a general spin-$\frac{1 {2}$ hadron, and then derive the exact full tree-level unpolarized differential cross sections of both (anti)neutrino-nucleon and (anti)neutrino-antinucleon elastic scatterings in the lab frame for experimental measurements.}}
elements.sent-2411-034: In Sec.~\ref{sec:The quantum phase-space formalism , we present the key ingredients of the quantum phase-space formalism.}
elements.sent-2411-035: We start our analysis in Sec.~\ref{sec:Breit frame distributions with the 3D Breit frame distributions of weak-neutral axial-vector four-current inside a spin-$\tfrac{1 {2}$ hadron.}}
elements.sent-2411-036: We then present in Sec.~\ref{sec:Elastic frame distributions the generic elastic frame distributions inside a moving spin-$\frac{1 {2}$ hadron at arbitrary average momenta.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: In Sec.~\ref{sec:Light-front distributions , we present the light-front distributions, and the explicit demonstration of light-front amplitudes via the proper infinite-momentum frame limit of elastic-frame amplitudes.}
elements.sent-2411-038: In Sec.~\ref{sec:Breit frame distributions to Sec.~\ref{sec:Light-front distributions , we also supply our rederived 3D and 2D transverse mean-square axial and spin radii.}}
elements.sent-2411-039: Finally, we summarize our findings in Sec.~\ref{sec:Summary .}
elements.sent-2411-040: For completeness, we also provide details for the parametrization of nucleon weak-neutral axial-vector form factors in Appendix~\ref{Appendix-A , and further discussions on the breakdown of Abel transformation in the case of axial charge distributions in Appendix~\ref{Appendix-B .}}
elements.sent-2411-041: The weak-neutral axial-vector FFs of a spin-$\tfrac{1 {2}$ hadron in the weak sector contain in general axial $G_A^Z$, induced pseudoscalar $G_P^Z$ and induced (pseudo-)tensor $G_T^Z$ FFs~\cite{Gourdin:1974iq,Ohlsson:1998bk,Bernard:2001rs,Aliev:2007qu , as real functions of spacelike four-momentum transfer squared $Q^2=-\Delta^2 \geq 0$ owing to the time-reversal ($\mathsf T$) invariance of weak interactions~\cite{Gourdin:1974iq,Barquilla-Cano:2002btm .}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: They together describe the internal weak-neutral axial-vector content of the system in response to external weak-neutral current interactions; see e.g., Fig.~\ref{Fig_FeynmanDiagram for the tree-level Feynman diagram of neutrino-nucleon elastic scattering.}
elements.figure-2411-001: {\includegraphics[angle=0,scale=0.52]{Nucleon_Feynman_Diagram_NC_v3n.pdf}} \caption{The tree-level Feynman diagram of the $t$-channel weak-neutral current elastic scattering reaction $\nu_\ell(k) + N(p) \to \nu_\ell(k') + N(p')$ in the first Born approximation (i.e. one virtual $Z^0$ boson exchange), with $\ell=e,\mu,\tau$. The four-momentum transfer is $\Delta=k-k'=p'-p$.} \label{Fig_FeynmanDiagram}
elements.sent-2411-043: In the case of a spin-$\tfrac{1 {2}$ hadron, matrix elements of the weak-neutral axial-vector four-current operator $\hat j_{5 ^{\mu (x)\equiv \hat{\bar \psi (x) \gamma^\mu \gamma^5 \hat\psi(x)$ in general can be parametrized as~\cite{Gourdin:1974iq,Ohlsson:1998bk,Bernard:2001rs,Aliev:2007qu \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{MatrElem-Axial-Z} \langle p',s'|\hat j_{5}^{\mu}(0)|p,s\rangle = \bar u(p',s')\left[ \gamma^\mu G_A^Z + \frac{\Delta^{\mu}}{2 M}G_P^Z + \frac{i\sigma^{\mu\nu}\Delta_\nu}{2M} G_T^Z \right] \gamma^5 u(p,s), \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\hat \psi(x)$ denotes the quark field operator (which can either be a flavor-singlet or flavor-multiplet), and $p^2=p'^2=M^2$ are the on-mass-shell relations for the same hadron $\text{B}$ of mass $M$ in the initial and final states.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-045: Since the explicit (diagonal) matrix forms of generators $T_a$ associated with the operator $\hat j_{5,a ^{\mu (x)\equiv \hat{\bar \psi (x) \gamma^\mu \gamma^5 T_a \hat\psi(x)$, see e.g. Ref.~\cite{Bernard:2001rs , for a given flavor-space fundamental representation group $SU(n)_f$ ($n \geq 2$ and $n \in \mathbb Z$) of a given (anti)neutrino-hadron elastic scattering reaction channel are known, it is convenient to work in the $U(1)_f$ representation without loss of generality.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: According to Weinberg~\cite{Weinberg:1958ut , one can use the $\mathsf{G}$-parity to classify all possible currents formed by Dirac bilinears in the literature in term of the first- and second-class currents.}
elements.sent-2411-047: The $\mathsf{G}$-parity is defined as the combination of the charge-conjugation $\mathsf{C}$-parity after an rotation of a $180^\circ$ angle around the $y$-axis in isospin space, namely \begin{equation}\label{G-parity} \mathsf{G} \equiv \mathsf{C}\, \exp\left( i\pi I_y \right),\end{equation} where $I_y= \sigma_y/2 $ is the $y$-component isospin matrix.
elements.sent-2411-048: As the ne plus ultra, one can easily demonstrate that the vector and axial-vector four-currents $j^\mu=\bar{\psi}\gamma^\mu \psi$ and $j^\mu_5=\bar{\psi}\gamma^\mu\gamma^5 \psi$ which transform in the following manner \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{G-VA-first} \mathsf{G} j^{\mu} \mathsf{G}^{-1} = +j^{\mu},\qquad \mathsf{G} j_5^{\mu} \mathsf{G}^{-1} = -j_5^{\mu} \end{aligned}\end{equation} are classified as the first-class currents, whereas those transform in the opposite manner \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{G-VA-second} \mathsf{G} j^{\mu} \mathsf{G}^{-1} = -j^{\mu},\qquad \mathsf{G} j_5^{\mu} \mathsf{G}^{-1} = +j_5^{\mu} \end{aligned}\end{equation} are classified as the second-class currents.
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}\label{G-VA-first} G j^{\mu} G^{-1} = +j^{\mu},\qquad G j_5^{\mu} G^{-1} = -j_5^{\mu} \end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}\label{G-VA-second} G j^{\mu} G^{-1} = -j^{\mu},\qquad G j_5^{\mu} G^{-1} = +j_5^{\mu} \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-049: For completeness, we do summarize in Table~\ref{Tab_CurrentClass the classification of all possible independent currents formed by Dirac bilinears under the $\mathsf{G}$-parity transformation.}

elements.sent-2411-054: According to the famous $V-A$ theory~\cite{Schwinger:1957em,Lee:1957jf,Feynman:1958ty in weak interactions, there are two types of currents involved in both weak-neutral and weak-charged current interactions: the weak vector current $\hat j^\mu(x) \equiv \hat{\bar \psi (x) \gamma^\mu \hat\psi(x) $ and the weak axial-vector current $\hat j_{5 ^\mu(x) \equiv \hat{\bar \psi (x) \gamma^\mu \gamma^5 \hat\psi(x) $.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: They together celebrate in the $V-A$ form for the total weak current of the system: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{MatrElem-VmA-Z} \langle p',s'|[ \hat j^\mu(0) - \hat j_5^\mu(0) ]|p,s\rangle = \bar u(p',s') \Gamma_{V-A}^\mu(p',p) u(p,s), \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\Gamma_{V-A ^\mu $ is the total vertex function.}

elements.sent-2411-057: Similarly, matrix elements of the weak-neutral vector four-current operator for a generic spin-$\frac{1 {2}$ target in general can be parametrized as~\cite{Ohlsson:1998bk,Singh:2006xp,Fatima:2018tzs,Lorce:2020onh,Chen:2023dxp,Holstein:1984ga \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{MatrElem-Vector-Z} \langle p',s'|\hat j^\mu(0) |p,s \rangle = \bar u(p',s') \left[ \frac{MP^\mu}{P^2} G_E^Z + \frac{i\epsilon^{\mu\alpha\beta \lambda} \Delta_\alpha P_\beta \gamma_\lambda \gamma^5 }{2P^2} G_M^Z + \frac{\Delta^\mu}{2M} G_S^Z \right]u(p,s), \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $P = (p'+p)/2$, $\Delta=p'-p$, $p^2 = p'^2 = M^2$ and $\epsilon_{0123 =+1$.}}}

elements.subsection-2411-003: Exact full tree-level unpolarized differential cross sections
elements.sent-2411-060: Since we have discussed the full vertex function (\ref{MatrElem-VmA-Z ) for a generic spin-$\frac{1 {2}$ hadron, we are now ready to connect these weak-neutral FFs $G_E^Z$, $G_M^Z$, $G_S^Z$, $G_A^Z$, $G_P^Z$ and $G_T^Z$ to the unpolarized differential cross sections measured in (anti)neutrino-nucleon elastic scattering experiments~\cite{Horstkotte:1981ne,Ahrens:1986xe,MiniBooNE:2010xqw,MiniBooNE:2013dds .}}}
elements.sent-2411-061: It should be noted that although the tree-level unpolarized differential cross sections of (anti)neutrino-nucleon elastic scatterings have been derived in Refs.~\cite{Weinberg:1972tu,Horstkotte:1981ne,Ahrens:1986xe,Garvey:1992cg,Garvey:1993sg,Sufian:2018qtw , the vertex function $\Gamma_{V-A ^\mu$ employed in Refs.~\cite{Weinberg:1972tu,Horstkotte:1981ne,Ahrens:1986xe,Garvey:1992cg,Garvey:1993sg,Sufian:2018qtw is however incomplete, missing usually the Lorentz structures associated with $G_T^Z$, $G_S^Z$ and even $G_P^Z$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: For neutrino-nucleon elastic scattering reaction (with $\ell=e,\mu,\tau$) \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \nu_\ell(k,r) + N(p,s) \to \nu_\ell(k',r') + N(p',s'), \end{aligned}\end{equation} the scattering amplitude $i\mathcal M$ in the Feynman gauge is given by \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} i\mathcal M = (-i\, \xi_C) \left( \frac{M_Z^2}{M_Z^2+Q^2} \right) \frac{G_F}{ \sqrt{2} } \ell_\mu J^\mu_\text{weak}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} with \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \ell_\mu \equiv \bar u(k',r')\gamma_\mu (1-\gamma^5) u(k,r), J^\mu_\text{weak} \equiv \bar u(p',s') \Gamma^\mu_{V-A} u(p,s), \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\xi_C=1$ (for weak-neutral currents), $M_Z\approx 91.1880(20)~\text{GeV}$ is the $Z^0$ boson mass~\cite{ParticleDataGroup:2024cfk , $G_F$ is the Fermi coupling constant, and $\Gamma^\mu_{V-A}$ is the full vertex function given in Eq.~(\ref{MatrElem-VmA-Z ), and we have used the fact that \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \frac{ G_F }{ \sqrt{2} } = \frac{g^2}{8M_Z^2 \cos^2 \theta_W}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} with $g$ the universal weak coupling constant and $\theta_W$ the Weinberg angle.}}

elements.sent-2411-071: It should be emphasized that our exact results of the full tree-level unpolarized differential cross sections (\ref{NC-Z-diffSgm , \ref{NC-Z-diffSgm-anti ) also apply to other spin-$\frac{1 {2}$ hadrons (including their antiparticles), e.g. $\Lambda^0$, $\bar\Lambda^0$, $\Sigma^0$, $\bar{\Sigma ^0$, $\Xi^0$, $\bar{\Xi ^0$, etc., where the full vertex functions (\ref{MatrElem-VmA-Z , \ref{MatrElem-VmA-Z-anti ) including $6$ FFs individually are explicitly taken into account.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-072: Relative to those previous studies~\cite{Weinberg:1972tu,Horstkotte:1981ne,Ahrens:1986xe,Garvey:1992cg,Garvey:1993sg,Sufian:2018qtw , our results (\ref{NC-Z-diffSgm ) of the full tree-level unpolarized differential cross sections are more general and complete, and should be more useful for ongoing and future elastic (anti)neutrino-nucleon scattering experiments~\cite{Horstkotte:1981ne,Ahrens:1986xe,MiniBooNE:2010xqw,MiniBooNE:2013dds .}}}
elements.sent-2411-073: Last but not least, we do realize during the derivations of the full tree-level unpolarized differential cross sections (\ref{NC-Z-diffSgm , \ref{NC-Z-diffSgm-anti ) that polarized differential cross sections of polarized (anti)neutrino-(anti)nucleon elastic scatterings will provide us more rich and complete information about nucleon weak-neutral vector and axial-vector FFs in the weak sector, where FFs $G_P^Z$ (or $\bar G_P^Z$) and $G_S^Z$ (or $\bar G_S^Z$) may explicitly appear in the polarized differential cross sections\footnote{We note that two virtual $Z^0$ bosons exchange (or multiple virtual $Z^0$ bosons exchange) and radiative corrections at one-loop order (or even higher loops) may also provide us the possibilities that nucleon FFs $G_P^Z$ (or $\bar G_P^Z$) and $G_S^Z$ (or $\bar G_S^Z$) may explicitly appear in the unpolarized differential cross sections.}}}
elements.sent-2411-074: Although FFs are objects defined in momentum space and extracted from experimental data involving particles with well-defined momenta, their physical interpretation actually resides in position space~\cite{Chen:2023dxp .}
elements.sent-2411-075: Because of Lorentz symmetry, the notion of relativistic spatial distributions in general depends on the target average momentum, hindering therefore in general a probabilistic interpretation in position space.
elements.sent-2411-076: We are therefore naturally led to switch our perspective to a phase-space picture, which is quasiprobabilistic at the quantum level owing to Heisenberg's uncertainty principle.
elements.sent-2411-077: In this section, we only present the key ingredients of the quantum phase-space formalism~\cite{Lorce:2020onh,Chen:2022smg,Chen:2023dxp .}
elements.sent-2411-078: In quantum field theory, it has been known for a long time that the expectation value of any an operator $\hat O$ in a physical state $|\Psi\rangle$ can be expressed as~\cite{Wigner:1932eb,Hillery:1983ms,Bialynicki-Birula:1991jwl \begin{equation}\label{PS-representation} \langle\Psi|\hat O(x)|\Psi\rangle=\sum_{s',s}\int\frac{\ud^3P}{(2\pi)^3}\,\ud^3R\,\rho^{s's}_\Psi(\uvec R,\uvec P)\,\langle\hat O\rangle^{s's}_{\uvec R,\uvec P}(x),\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \rho^{s's}_\Psi(\uvec R,\uvec P) \equiv\int\ud^3z\,e^{-i\uvec P\cdot\uvec z}\,\Psi^*(\uvec R - \tfrac{\uvec z}{2},s')\Psi(\uvec R + \tfrac{\uvec z}{2},s) =\int\frac{\ud^3q}{(2\pi)^3}\,e^{-i\uvec q\cdot\uvec R}\,\widetilde\Psi^*(\uvec P+\tfrac{\uvec q}{2},s')\widetilde\Psi(\uvec P-\tfrac{\uvec q}{2},s) \end{aligned}\end{equation} defines the Wigner distribution interpreted as the quantum weight for finding the system at average position $\uvec R=\tfrac{1 {2 (\uvec r'+\uvec r)$ and average momentum $\uvec P=\tfrac{1 {2 (\uvec p'+\uvec p)$.}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-024: \begin{equation} \rho^{s's}_\Psi(\uvec R,\uvec P) \equiv\int\ud^3z\,e^{-i\uvec P\cdot\uvec z}\,\Psi^*(\uvec R - \tfrac{\uvec z}{2},s')\Psi(\uvec R + \tfrac{\uvec z}{2},s) =\int\frac{\ud^3q}{(2\pi)^3}\,e^{-i\uvec q\cdot\uvec R}\,\widetilde\Psi^*(\uvec P+\tfrac{\uvec q}{2},s')\widetilde\Psi(\uvec P-\tfrac{\uvec q}{2},s) \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-079: Here, $\uvec z\equiv\uvec r-\uvec r'$ and $\uvec q\equiv \uvec p'-\uvec p$ denote the relative position and relative momentum, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-080: We note that $\widetilde\Psi(\uvec p,s)\equiv\langle p,s|\Psi\rangle/\sqrt{2p^0}$ defines the momentum-space wavepacket of the system, and satisfies the following completeness condition \begin{equation}\sum_s\int\frac{\ud^3p}{(2\pi)^3}\,|\widetilde\Psi(\uvec p,s)|^2=1,\end{equation} where $|p,s\rangle$ denote the four-momentum eigenstates and are covariantly normalized as $\langle p',s'|p,s\rangle = 2p^0 (2\pi)^3 \delta^{(3) (\uvec p'-\uvec p) \delta_{s's}$.}
elements.sent-2411-081: Both initial and final four-momenta being on-mass-shell $p^2=p'^2=M^2$, the energy components are given by $p^0=\sqrt{\uvec p^2+M^2}$ and $p'^0=\sqrt{\uvec p'^2+M^2}$.
elements.sent-2411-082: Accordingly, $\Psi(\uvec r,s)\equiv\langle \uvec r,s|\Psi\rangle$ defines the position-space wave packet at $t=0$ via the Newton-Wigner position operator~\cite{Pryce:1948pf,Newton:1949cq,Foldy:1949wa,Pavsic:2017orp , whose eigenstates at $t=0$ are related to momentum eigenstates via Fourier transforms \begin{equation}|\uvec r,s\rangle=\int\frac{\ud^3p}{(2\pi)^3}\,e^{-i\uvec p\cdot\uvec r}\,\frac{|p,s\rangle}{\sqrt{2p^0}}\end{equation} and are normalized as $\langle \uvec r',s'|\uvec r,s\rangle=\delta^{(3) (\uvec r'-\uvec r)\delta_{s's}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-083: One finds that the position-space wave packet at $t=0$ is related to the momentum-space one via the familiar Fourier transform \begin{equation}\Psi(\uvec r,s)=\int\frac{\ud^3p}{(2\pi)^3}\,e^{i\uvec p\cdot\uvec r}\,\widetilde\Psi(\uvec p,s)\end{equation} and satisfies the following completeness condition \begin{equation}\sum_s\int\ud^3r\,|\Psi(\uvec r,s)|^2=1.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-084: In a relativistic theory, the Newton-Wigner position operator~\cite{Pryce:1948pf,Newton:1949cq,Foldy:1949wa,Pavsic:2017orp is, to the best of our knowledge, the only 3D position operator satisfying usual commutation relations with both linear and angular momentum operators, and having mutually commuting components~\cite{Lorce:2018zpf,Lorce:2021gxs .}}
elements.sent-2411-085: Although this operator does not transform as part of a Lorentz four-vector, it allows one to localize a relativistic system at a fixed time.
elements.sent-2411-086: Probabilistic densities are then recovered upon integration over either average position or momentum variables \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \int\ud^3R\,\rho_\Psi^{s's}(\uvec R,\uvec P) =\widetilde\Psi^*(\uvec P,s')\widetilde\Psi(\uvec P,s), \int\frac{\ud^3P}{(2\pi)^3}\,\rho_\Psi^{s's}(\uvec R,\uvec P) =\Psi^*(\uvec R,s')\Psi(\uvec R,s). \end{aligned}\end{equation} A compelling feature of the quantum phase-space formalism is that wave-packet details have been cleanly factored out in Eq.~\eqref{PS-representation .}
elements.formula-2411-029: \begin{equation} \int\ud^3R\,\rho_\Psi^{s's}(\uvec R,\uvec P) =\widetilde\Psi^*(\uvec P,s')\widetilde\Psi(\uvec P,s), \int\frac{\ud^3P}{(2\pi)^3}\,\rho_\Psi^{s's}(\uvec R,\uvec P) =\Psi^*(\uvec R,s')\Psi(\uvec R,s). \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-087: We can then interpret the phase-space amplitude \begin{equation}\label{internal-distribution} \langle\hat O\rangle^{s's}_{\uvec R,\uvec P}(x)=\int\frac{\ud^3\Delta}{(2\pi)^3}\,e^{i\uvec\Delta\cdot\uvec R}\,\frac{\langle P+\tfrac{\Delta}{2},s'|\hat O(x)|P-\tfrac{\Delta}{2},s\rangle}{2\sqrt{p'^0p^0}}\end{equation} as the internal distribution associated with a state localized in the Wigner sense around average position $\uvec R$ and average momentum $\uvec P$~\cite{Lorce:2018zpf,Lorce:2018egm,Lorce:2021gxs,Lorce:2020onh,Chen:2022smg,Chen:2023dxp , with $P \cdot \Delta=0$.}
elements.sent-2411-088: From a phase-space perspective, the BF can be regarded as the average rest frame of the system, where spin structure assumes its simplest form~\cite{Lorce:2020onh,Lorce:2022jyi,Chen:2022odn,Chen:2023dxp,Chen:2024oxx .}
elements.sent-2411-089: In this frame, one can provide fully relativistic 3D spatial distributions of a generic composite system for its static internal structures in the Wigner sense.
elements.sent-2411-090: This is also the reason why many 3D mean-square radii of a hadron, e.g. (electric) charge $\langle r_\text{ch ^2 \rangle$, mass $\langle r_\text{mass ^2 \rangle$, mechanical $\langle r_\text{mech ^2 \rangle$ and spin $\langle r_\text{spin ^2 \rangle$ radii, are usually defined in this frame~\cite{Gao:2021sml,Polyakov:2018guq,Polyakov:2018zvc,Burkert:2023wzr,Hackett:2023rif,Chen:2023dxp,Chen:2024oxx .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-091: The BF is specified by the condition $\uvec P=\uvec 0$, which physically constrains that the energy transfer $\Delta^0=\uvec P \cdot \uvec\Delta/P^0$ vanishes automatically in this frame.
elements.sent-2411-092: As a result, internal distributions in the BF do not dependent on time $x^0$ arising due to translation invariance of the matrix elements in Eq.~(\ref{internal-distribution ).}
elements.sent-2411-093: Relativistic three-dimensional BF distributions are therefore in general defined as~\cite{Chen:2023dxp \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{BF-def} O_B(\uvec r) \equiv \langle \hat O \rangle_{\uvec 0, \uvec 0}^{s_B' s_B}(\uvec r) = \int\frac{\ud^3\Delta}{(2\pi)^3}\, e^{-i\uvec\Delta \cdot \uvec r}\, \frac{\langle p_B', s_B'| \hat O(0)|p_B, s_B \rangle }{2P_B^0}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\uvec r = \uvec x -\uvec R$ is the distance relative to the center $\uvec R=\uvec 0$ of the system, $\uvec p_B'=-\uvec p_B = \uvec\Delta/2$, $Q^2=\uvec\Delta^2$, $\tau \equiv Q^2/(4M^2)$ and $P_B^0=p_B^{\prime\, 0 =p_B^0=M\sqrt{1+\tau}$.}}
elements.formula-2411-031: \begin{equation}\label{BF-def} O_B(\uvec r) \equiv \langle \hat O \rangle_{\uvec 0, \uvec 0}^{s_B' s_B}(\uvec r) = \int\frac{\ud^3\Delta}{(2\pi)^3}\, e^{-i\uvec\Delta \cdot \uvec r}\, \frac{\langle p_B', s_B'| \hat O(0)|p_B, s_B \rangle }{2P_B^0}, \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-094: Evaluating the matrix elements (\ref{MatrElem-Axial-Z ) in the BF leads to~\cite{Meissner:1986js,Ohlsson:1998bk,Barquilla-Cano:2002btm,Schindler:2006jq,Chen:2024oxx \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{BF-amplitudes} \mathcal A^0_B = \sqrt{1 + \tau }\,(\uvec \sigma \cdot \uvec\Delta) G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta^2), \uvec{\mathcal A}_B = 2P_B^0 \left[ \uvec\sigma - \frac{ \uvec\Delta (\uvec\Delta \cdot \uvec\sigma ) }{4P_B^0(P_B^0+M) } \right] G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta^2) - \frac{ \uvec\Delta (\uvec\Delta \cdot \uvec\sigma ) }{ 2M } G_P^Z(\uvec\Delta^2), \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\mathcal A_B^\mu \equiv \langle p_B',s_B'|\hat j_{5 ^{\mu (0)|p_B,s_B\rangle$, and explicit canonical polarization indices will be omitted for better legibility hereafter unless necessary.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-095: Applying the general definition (\ref{BF-def ) to the axial-vector four-current operator with the help of BF amplitudes (\ref{BF-amplitudes ), one obtains the following relativistic 3D BF weak-neutral axial-vector four-current distributions~\cite{Chen:2024oxx \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{3DBF-distributions} J_{5,B}^0(\uvec r) = \int\frac{\ud^3\Delta}{(2\pi)^3}\, e^{-i\uvec\Delta \cdot \uvec r}\, \frac{\uvec \sigma \cdot \uvec\Delta}{2M} G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta^2) = i \frac{\uvec\sigma\cdot \uvec\nabla_{\uvec r} }{2M}\int\frac{\ud^3\Delta}{(2\pi)^3}\, e^{-i\uvec\Delta \cdot \uvec r}\, G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta^2), \uvec J_{5,B}(\uvec r) = \int\frac{\ud^3\Delta}{(2\pi)^3}\, e^{-i\uvec\Delta \cdot \uvec r}\, \bigg\{ \left[ \uvec\sigma - \frac{ \uvec\Delta (\uvec\Delta \cdot \uvec\sigma ) }{4P_B^0(P_B^0+M) } \right]G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta^2) - \frac{\uvec\Delta (\uvec\Delta \cdot \uvec\sigma ) }{ 4M P_B^0 } G_P^Z(\uvec\Delta^2) \bigg\}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} We do observe that the 3D BF axial charge distribution $J_{5,B ^0(\uvec r)$ is in fact related to the weak-neutral induced (pseudo-)tensor FF $G_T^Z(Q^2)$ rather than the axial FF $G_A^Z(Q^2)$.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-033: \begin{equation}\label{3DBF-distributions} J_{5,B}^0(\uvec r) = \int\frac{\ud^3\Delta}{(2\pi)^3}\, e^{-i\uvec\Delta \cdot \uvec r}\, \frac{\uvec \sigma \cdot \uvec\Delta}{2M} G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta^2) = i \frac{\uvec\sigma\cdot \uvec\nabla_{\uvec r} }{2M}\int\frac{\ud^3\Delta}{(2\pi)^3}\, e^{-i\uvec\Delta \cdot \uvec r}\, G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta^2), \uvec J_{5,B}(\uvec r) = \int\frac{\ud^3\Delta}{(2\pi)^3}\, e^{-i\uvec\Delta \cdot \uvec r}\, \bigg\{ \left[ \uvec\sigma - \frac{ \uvec\Delta (\uvec\Delta \cdot \uvec\sigma ) }{4P_B^0(P_B^0+M) } \right]G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta^2) - \frac{\uvec\Delta (\uvec\Delta \cdot \uvec\sigma ) }{ 4M P_B^0 } G_P^Z(\uvec\Delta^2) \bigg\}. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-096: We stress that above results (\ref{BF-amplitudes ,\ref{3DBF-distributions ) are also well consistent with our previous work~\cite{Chen:2024oxx , where the $\mathsf{G}$-parity invariance of QCD\footnote{In strong interactions or QCD, e.g. the strong decays of mesons, the $\mathsf{G}$-parity invariance is exact.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-097: However, the $\mathsf{G}$-parity is in general not conserved in electromagnetic and weak interactions due to either quark mass or quark (electric) charge differences~\cite{Shiomi:1996np .}
elements.sent-2411-098: The Fourier transform of $\uvec\Delta G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta^2)$ in Eq.~(\ref{3DBF-distributions ) will lead to a purely imaginary BF axial charge distribution $J_{5,B ^0(\uvec r)$, since $G_T^Z(Q^2)$ is real by assuming the $\mathsf T$-invariance of weak interactions~\cite{LlewellynSmith:1971uhs,Gourdin:1974iq,Barquilla-Cano:2002btm,Fatima:2018tzs .}}}
elements.sent-2411-099: Besides, $J_{5,B ^0(\uvec r)$ is also parity-odd along the polarization axial owing to the associated parity-odd factor $(\uvec\sigma \cdot \uvec r)$ coming from the Fourier transform of $(\uvec\sigma \cdot \uvec \Delta)$.}
elements.sent-2411-100: In Fig.~\ref{Fig_3DBFNC_J0 , we illustrate the BF axial charge distributions $iJ_{5,B ^0(\uvec r) = -\text{Im}\left[ J_{5,B ^0(\uvec r) \right] $ and $4\pi r^2 \cdot iJ_{5,B ^0(\uvec r) $ with $r=|\uvec r|$ along the $z$-axis inside a longitudinally polarized proton, using the proton weak-neutral induced (pseudo-)tensor FF $G_T^Z(Q^2)$ given in Appendix~\ref{Appendix-A .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-101: For more details of the nucleon weak-neutral axial-vector FFs, see Appendix~\ref{Appendix-A .}
elements.figure-2411-003: {\includegraphics[angle=0,scale=0.60]{Nucleon_3DBFNCJ0_v3n.pdf}} \caption{BF weak-neutral axial charge distributions $iJ_{5,B}^0(\uvec r) = -\text{Im}[ J_{5,B}^0(\uvec r) ] $ (upper panel) and $4\pi r^2 \cdot iJ_{5,B}^0(\uvec r) $ (lower panel) along the $z$-axis inside a longitudinally polarized (i.e. $\hvec s \equiv \uvec\sigma_{s's} = \uvec e_z$) proton, using the proton weak-neutral induced (pseudo-)tensor FF $G_T^Z(Q^2)$ given in Appendix~\ref{Appendix-A}.} \label{Fig_3DBFNC_J0}
elements.sent-2411-102: On the other hand, we notice that the 3D BF axial-vector current distribution $\uvec J_{5,B}$ is in fact independent of the induced tensor form factor $G_T^Z(Q^2)$.
elements.sent-2411-103: In other words, $\uvec J_{5,B}$ is free from the second-class current contribution, and therefore assumes the same expression as in Ref.~\cite{Chen:2024oxx .}
elements.sent-2411-104: Using the QCD equation of motion~\cite{Leader:2013jra , one can show that~\cite{Chen:2024oxx \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{3DBF-spin-density} \uvec S_B(\uvec r) = \frac{1}{2}\uvec J_{5,B}(\uvec r) \end{aligned}\end{equation} is the physically meaningful 3D spin distribution in the BF, characterizing how the spin is distributed in the weak neutral sector.}}
elements.sent-2411-105: Based on this meaningful and well-defined spin distribution (\ref{3DBF-spin-density ), one can derive the meaningful 3D mean-square spin radius $\langle r_\text{spin ^2 \rangle $~\cite{Chen:2024oxx , which relies on both the slope of the axial FF $G_A^Z(Q^2)$ at $Q^2=0$ and the ratio $G_P^Z(0)/G_A^Z(0)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-106: Using the proton weak-neutral axial-vector FFs $G_A^Z(Q^2)$ and $G_P^Z(Q^2)$ given in Appendix~\ref{Appendix-A , we find that the weak-neutral mean-square spin radius of the proton is given by~\cite{Chen:2024oxx \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{3DBF-mean-square-radii} \langle r_\text{spin}^2 \rangle^p = \frac{1}{G_A^Z(0)}\left[ -\uvec\nabla_{\uvec\Delta}^2 G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta^2) \right] \Big|_{\uvec\Delta = \uvec 0} + \frac{1}{4M^2}\left[ 1 + \frac{2 G_P^Z(0) }{ G_A^Z(0) } \right] \equiv r_S^2 \approx (2.1054~\text{fm})^2, \end{aligned}\end{equation} where the last term $\left( \frac{1 {2M^2 \frac{G_P^Z(0) { G_A^Z(0) \right)$ plays the dominant role since the ratio $G_P^Z(0)/G_A^Z(0) = (2M_p/M_\pi)^2 \approx 180.772$ is very large; see Appendix~\ref{Appendix-A for more details of $G_A^Z(Q^2)$ and $G_P^Z(Q^2)$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-107: In Fig.~\ref{Fig_3DBFNC_Jv , we illustrate the 3D BF weak-neutral axial-vector current distribution $\uvec J_{5,B (\uvec r)$ in the transverse plane inside a transversely polarized proton, using the proton weak-neutral axial-vector FFs $G_A^Z(Q^2)$ and $G_P^Z(Q^2)$ given in Appendix~\ref{Appendix-A .}}}
elements.sent-2411-108: We observe that the distribution is perfectly mirror symmetric (antisymmetric) with respect to the $x$-axis ($y$-axis) in the transverse plane.
elements.sent-2411-109: This can be more easily understood from the multipole decomposition~\cite{Chen:2023dxp of $\uvec J_{5,B (\uvec r)$, where we find that $\uvec J_{5,B (\uvec r)$ consists of mirror symmetric (antisymmetric) monopole and quadrupole contributions solely with respect to the $x$-axis ($y$-axis).}}}
elements.figure-2411-004: {\includegraphics[angle=0,scale=0.55]{Nucleon_3DBFNCJv_v3n.pdf}} \caption{The BF weak-neutral axial-vector current distribution $\uvec J_{5,B}(\uvec r)$ in the transverse plane inside a transversely polarized (i.e. $\hvec s = \uvec e_x$) proton, using the proton weak-neutral axial-vector FFs $G_A^Z(Q^2)$ and $G_P^Z(Q^2)$ given in Appendix~\ref{Appendix-A}.} \label{Fig_3DBFNC_Jv}
elements.sent-2411-110: Recalling in the BF that $\uvec p_B'=-\uvec p_B=\uvec\Delta/2$ and $p_B^{\prime\, 0 =p_B^0 =P_B^0$, we do recognize in Eq.~(\ref{BF-amplitudes ) the characteristic spin structure $\left(\uvec\sigma - \frac{\uvec p_B(\uvec p_B\cdot \uvec\sigma) {p_B^0(p_B^0+M) \right)$ defined relative to the center of mass $\uvec R_M = \uvec R = \uvec 0$, which is the only relativistic center transforming as the spatial part of a Lorentz four-vector, and corresponds therefore to the true relativistic center of the system~\cite{Lorce:2018zpf,Lorce:2021gxs,Chen:2023dxp .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-111: The other two relativistic centers are the center of energy $\uvec R_E=\uvec R_M + \frac{\uvec P \times \hvec s {2M(E_P+M) $ and the center of canonical spin $\uvec R_c=\uvec R_M + \frac{\uvec P \times \hvec s {2M E_P}$ (the pivot about which the internal angular momentum takes the same value as in the rest frame), where $E_P \equiv \sqrt{\uvec P^2 + M^2 $ and $\hvec s$ is a unit polarization vector.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-112: These two relativistic centers $\uvec R_E$ and $\uvec R_c$, however, will suffer from respectively sideways shifts $\frac{\uvec P \times \hvec s {2M(E_P+M)}$ and $\frac{\uvec P \times \hvec s {2M E_P}$ of pure relativistic origin when $(\uvec P \times \hvec s) \neq \uvec 0$, e.g, when the spinning system is longitudinally moving while it is transversely polarized.}}
elements.sent-2411-113: This in turn justifies that the parametrization of the axial-vector four-current operator in Eq.~(\ref{MatrElem-Axial-Z ) is indeed physically clear and transparent.}
elements.sent-2411-114: For more details of the relativistic centers and sideways shifts, see Refs.~\cite{Lorce:2018zpf,Lorce:2021gxs and also the similar discussions for the parametrization of polarization-magnetization tensor in Ref.~\cite{Chen:2023dxp .}}
elements.sent-2411-115: Note that the total axial charge for a generic spin-$\frac{1 {2}$ composite system in average at rest vanishes identically~\cite{Chen:2024oxx : \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \int \ud^3 r\, J_{5,B}^0(\uvec r) = \lim_{\uvec\Delta \to \uvec 0}\, \frac{1}{2M}\left[ (\uvec\sigma \cdot \uvec\Delta) G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta^2) \right] = 0, \end{aligned}\end{equation} because the term $(\uvec\sigma \cdot \uvec\Delta)$ in momentum-space is parity-odd, or equivalently the term $(\uvec\sigma \cdot \uvec r)$ in position-space is parity-odd.}}
elements.sent-2411-116: This means that the definition of standard mean-square axial (charge) radius \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{3DBF-MS-axial-radius} \langle r_A^2 \rangle \equiv \frac{\int \ud^3r\, r^2\,J_{5,B}^0(\uvec r) }{ \int \ud^3r\, J_{5,B}^0(\uvec r) } \end{aligned}\end{equation} for a generic spin-$\frac{1 {2}$ system is in fact not well-defined.}
elements.sent-2411-117: This is different from case for the definition of the mean-square charge radius of the neutron where one can replace $G_E^n(0)=0$ with $G_E^p(0)=1$~\cite{Gao:2021sml,Chen:2023dxp so as to make the definition well-defined\footnote{Note also the fact that the BF charge distribution of the neutron is spherically symmetric~\cite{Lorce:2020onh,Chen:2023dxp .}}}
elements.sent-2411-118: In the case when $G_T^Z(Q^2)=0$ by using $\mathsf G$-parity invariance of QCD or exact isospin symmetry, the 3D axial density $J_{5,B ^0(\uvec r)$ vanishes identically for a generic spin-$\frac{1 {2}$ target and thus the axial (charge) radius does not exist~\cite{Chen:2024oxx , contrary to what is usually stated in the literature~\cite{Meissner:1986xg,Meissner:1986js,Bernard:1992ys,A1:1999kwj,Meyer:2016oeg,Hill:2017wgb,MINERvA:2023avz,Petti:2023abz,Kaiser:2024vbc via \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{naive-3DMS-axial-radius} R_A^2 \equiv -\frac{6}{G_A^Z(0) }\frac{\ud G_A^Z(Q^2) }{\ud Q^2} \bigg|_{Q^2=0}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} In conclusion, in either $G_T^Z(Q^2) = 0$ or $G_T^Z(Q^2) \neq 0$ cases, the genuine 3D mean-square axial (charge) radius $\langle r_A^2 \rangle $ is not defined via the slope of the axial FF $G_A^Z(Q^2)$ at $Q^2=0$, since the 3D axial charge distribution itself is related to $G_T^Z(Q^2)$ rather than $G_A^Z(Q^2)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-119: Before we move forward, there are two key points deserving to be emphasized~\cite{Chen:2024oxx .}
elements.sent-2411-120: The first point is that the relation between a genuine 3D mean-square radius and the slope of the corresponding FF is in general not so obvious and simple, see the examples given in Refs.~\cite{Polyakov:2018zvc,Chen:2024oxx .}
elements.sent-2411-121: One needs to carefully define first the corresponding distribution, and then derive the genuine mean-square radius based on that distribution.
elements.sent-2411-122: The second point is that the concept of identifying a mean-square radius simply via the slope of the corresponding FF is in general misleading and incorrect.
elements.sent-2411-123: A typical example is the 3D mean-square axial (charge) radius which is not even related to the axial FF $G_A^Z(Q^2)$, see Eq.~(\ref{3DBF-distributions ) and Ref.~\cite{Chen:2024oxx .}}
elements.sent-2411-124: On the other hand, it also explicitly reveals the breakdown of Abel transformation in the case of axial charge distributions, see further discussions in Appendix~\ref{Appendix-B .}
elements.sent-2411-125: BF distributions provide us the best proxy for picturing a system in 3D sitting in average at rest around the origin in the Wigner sense, where the spin in the parametrization (\ref{MatrElem-Axial-Z ) is defined with respect to the relativistic center of mass.}
elements.sent-2411-126: If one is however interested in the internal structure of a moving system, one can employ the so-called elastic frame (EF) distributions introduced in Ref.~\cite{Lorce:2017wkb .}
elements.sent-2411-127: Accordingly, the relativistic 2D EF distributions are in general unambiguously defined as~\cite{Lorce:2020onh,Chen:2023dxp \begin{equation}\label{EF-def} \begin{aligned} O_\text{EF}(\uvec b_\perp;P_z) \equiv \int \ud r_z\, \langle \hat O \rangle_{\uvec 0, \uvec P}^{s's}(\uvec r) = \int \frac{\ud^2 \Delta_\perp}{(2\pi)^2}\, e^{-i\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec b_\perp }\, \frac{\langle p',s'| \hat O(0) |p,s \rangle }{2P^0} \bigg|_{\Delta_z=|\uvec P_\perp|=0}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} where the $z$-axis has been chosen along $\uvec P=(\uvec 0_\perp, P_z)$ without loss of generality, and $\uvec r=\uvec x-\uvec R=(\uvec b_\perp, r_z)$ is the position relative to the center $\uvec R=\uvec 0$ of the system.}
elements.formula-2411-039: \begin{equation}\label{EF-def}  O_EF(\uvec b_\perp;P_z) \equiv \int \ud r_z\, \langle \hat O \rangle_{\uvec 0, \uvec P}^{s's}(\uvec r) = \int \frac{\ud^2 \Delta_\perp}{(2\pi)^2}\, e^{-i\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec b_\perp }\, \frac{\langle p',s'| \hat O(0) |p,s \rangle }{2P^0} \bigg|_{\Delta_z=|\uvec P_\perp|=0}, \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-128: Integrating over the longitudinal coordinate $r_z$ amounts to setting the longitudinal momentum transfer $\Delta_z$ to zero, which in turn implies a vanishing energy transfer $\Delta^0 = \uvec P \cdot \uvec \Delta/P^0=0$ and hence time-independent distributions.
elements.sent-2411-129: At $P_z = 0$, the EF distributions (\ref{EF-def ) coincide with the BF distributions (\ref{BF-def ) projected onto the transverse plane \begin{equation}\label{EF-BF-limit} \begin{aligned} O_\text{EF}(\uvec b_\perp;0) = \int \ud r_z\, O_B(\uvec r) = \int\frac{\ud^2\Delta_\perp}{(2\pi)^2}\, e^{-i\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec b_\perp}\, \frac{\langle p_B', s_B'| \hat O(0)|p_B, s_B \rangle }{2P_B^0} \bigg|_{\Delta_z=|\uvec P|=0}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} In the limit $P_z \to \infty$, the EF distributions (\ref{EF-def ) explicitly reproduce the IMF distributions \begin{equation}\label{IMF-def} \begin{aligned} O_\text{IMF}(\uvec b_\perp) \equiv \lim_{P_z \to \infty}\, O_\text{EF}(\uvec b_\perp;P_z) = \int \frac{\ud^2 \Delta_\perp}{(2\pi)^2}\, e^{-i\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec b_\perp }\, \frac{\langle p',s'| \hat O(0) |p,s \rangle }{2P^0} \bigg|_{\Delta_z=0,P_z \to \infty}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} which coincide most of the time with the corresponding LF distributions, up to some trivial factors~\cite{Lorce:2020onh,Chen:2022smg,Chen:2023dxp,Chen:2024oxx,Lorce:2018egm,Lorce:2022jyi,Kim:2021kum,Kim:2022bia,Hong:2023tkv .}}}}
elements.formula-2411-040: \begin{equation}\label{EF-BF-limit}  O_EF(\uvec b_\perp;0) = \int \ud r_z\, O_B(\uvec r) = \int\frac{\ud^2\Delta_\perp}{(2\pi)^2}\, e^{-i\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec b_\perp}\, \frac{\langle p_B', s_B'| \hat O(0)|p_B, s_B \rangle }{2P_B^0} \bigg|_{\Delta_z=|\uvec P|=0}. \end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-041: \begin{equation}\label{IMF-def}  O_IMF(\uvec b_\perp) \equiv \lim_{P_z \to \infty}\, O_EF(\uvec b_\perp;P_z) = \int \frac{\ud^2 \Delta_\perp}{(2\pi)^2}\, e^{-i\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec b_\perp }\, \frac{\langle p',s'| \hat O(0) |p,s \rangle }{2P^0} \bigg|_{\Delta_z=0,P_z \to \infty}, \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-130: EF distribution provides therefore a smooth and physically clear interpolation between the BF and LF distributions.
elements.sent-2411-131: Evaluating directly the matrix elements (\ref{MatrElem-Axial-Z ) in the generic EF leads to~\cite{Chen:2024oxx \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{EF-amplitudes} \mathcal A_\text{EF}^0 = 2P_z \sigma_z G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \frac{P^0}{M} (\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec\sigma_\perp) G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2), \mathcal A_\text{EF}^z = 2P^0 \sigma_z G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \frac{P_z}{M} (\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec\sigma_\perp) G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2), \uvec{\mathcal A}_{\text{EF}}^\perp = 2\sqrt{P^2} \left[ \frac{ P^0+M(1+\tau) }{ (P^0+M)\sqrt{1+\tau} } \uvec\sigma_\perp + \frac{(\uvec e_z \times i\uvec\Delta_\perp)_\perp }{2M} \frac{P_z }{ (P^0+M)\sqrt{1+\tau} } \right] G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \qquad- \frac{\uvec\Delta_\perp( \uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec\sigma_\perp) }{2}\left[ \frac{ G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) }{ P^0+M } + \frac{ G_P^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) }{ M } \right] , \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\mathcal A_\text{EF ^\mu \equiv \langle p',s'|\hat j_{5 ^{\mu (0)|p,s\rangle$, $p'=(P^0, \uvec\Delta_\perp/2, P_z)$, $p=(P^0, -\uvec\Delta_\perp/2, P_z)$, $Q^2=\uvec\Delta_\perp^2$, and $P^0 = \sqrt{M^2(1+\tau)+P_z^2}$.}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-042: \begin{equation}\label{EF-amplitudes} \mathcal A_EF^0 = 2P_z \sigma_z G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \frac{P^0}{M} (\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec\sigma_\perp) G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2), \mathcal A_EF^z = 2P^0 \sigma_z G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \frac{P_z}{M} (\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec\sigma_\perp) G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2), \uvec{\mathcal A}_{EF}^\perp = 2\sqrt{P^2} \left[ \frac{ P^0+M(1+\tau) }{ (P^0+M)\sqrt{1+\tau} } \uvec\sigma_\perp + \frac{(\uvec e_z \times i\uvec\Delta_\perp)_\perp }{2M} \frac{P_z }{ (P^0+M)\sqrt{1+\tau} } \right] G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \qquad- \frac{\uvec\Delta_\perp( \uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec\sigma_\perp) }{2}\left[ \frac{ G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) }{ P^0+M } + \frac{ G_P^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) }{ M } \right] , \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-132: Poincar\'e symmetry can also be employed to determine how the matrix elements of the axial-vector four-current operator in different Lorentz frames are related to each other.
elements.formula-2411-043: \begin{equation}\label{covariant-Lorentz-Transf} \langle p',s'|\hat j_5^\mu(0)|p,s\rangle = \sum_{s_{B}',s_{B} } D^{\dag(j)}_{s' s_{B}'}(p_{B}',\Lambda) D^{(j)}_{s_{B}s}(p_{B},\Lambda)\, \Lambda^{\mu}_{\phantom{\mu}\nu} \, \langle p_{B}',s_{B}'|\hat j_5^{\nu}(0)|p_{B},s_{B}\rangle, \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-134: Alternatively, one can analytically reproduce above EF amplitudes (\ref{EF-amplitudes ) by applying the covariant Lorentz transformation (\ref{covariant-Lorentz-Transf ) on the BF amplitudes (\ref{BF-amplitudes ) at $\Delta_z=0$, where the Wigner rotation matrix $D^{(1/2)}$ is explicitly given by~\cite{Chen:2022smg,Chen:2023dxp \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{Wigner-D} D^{(1/2)}(p_B,\Lambda) = D^{\dag(1/2)}(p_B',\Lambda) = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\frac{\theta}{2} -e^{-i\phi_\Delta}\sin\frac{\theta}{2} e^{i\phi_\Delta } \sin\frac{\theta}{2} \cos\frac{\theta}{2} \end{pmatrix} \end{aligned}\end{equation} with $\uvec\Delta=Q(\cos\phi_\Delta, \sin\phi_\Delta,0)$ and $\theta$ being the Wigner rotation angle.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-135: We indeed explicitly reproduce our previous results in electromagnetic four-current, polarization-magnetization tensor and axial-vector four-current (without the second-class current contribution) cases for the Wigner angular conditions:~\cite{Lorce:2022jyi,Chen:2022smg,Chen:2023dxp,Chen:2024oxx \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{Wigner-angles} \cos\theta = \frac{ P^0 + M(1+\tau) }{ (P^0+M)\sqrt{1+\tau} }, \sin\theta = -\frac{\sqrt{\tau} P_z }{ (P^0+M)\sqrt{1+\tau} }. \end{aligned}\end{equation} As a result, the EF amplitudes (\ref{EF-amplitudes ) can be equivalently rewritten as \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{EF-amplitudes-2} \mathcal A_\text{EF}^0 = 2 M\gamma \sqrt{1+\tau} \left[ \beta \sigma_z G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \frac{ (\uvec\sigma_\perp \cdot \uvec\Delta_\perp) }{2M} G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \right], \mathcal A_\text{EF}^z = 2 M\gamma \sqrt{1+\tau} \left[ \sigma_z G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \beta \frac{ (\uvec\sigma_\perp \cdot \uvec\Delta_\perp) }{2M} G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \right], \uvec{\mathcal A}_{\text{EF}}^\perp = 2M \sqrt{1+\tau} \left\lbrace \uvec\sigma_\perp + \frac{(i\uvec\Delta_\perp \times \uvec e_z )_\perp }{|\uvec\Delta_\perp |} \left[ \sin\theta + \frac{( i\uvec\Delta_\perp \times \uvec\sigma)_z }{|\uvec\Delta_\perp|} (\cos\theta-1) \right] \right\rbrace G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \qquad - \frac{ \uvec\Delta_\perp( \uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec\sigma_\perp) }{2M} \left[ \frac{ G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) }{ 1 + \sqrt{1+\tau} } + G_P^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \right], \end{aligned}\end{equation} with~\cite{Chen:2022smg,Chen:2023dxp \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{gamma-beta} \gamma = \gamma(Q) =\frac{P^0}{ M\sqrt{1+\tau} } = \frac{P^0}{\sqrt{P^2} },\qquad \beta = \beta(Q)=\frac{P_z}{P^0}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\uvec{\mathcal A _{\text{EF ^\perp$ can also be alternatively but equivalently rewritten as~\cite{Chen:2024oxx \begin{equation}\label{EF-amplitudes-T2} \begin{aligned} \uvec{\mathcal A}_{\text{EF}}^\perp = 2\sqrt{P^2} \left[ \uvec\sigma_\perp \cos\theta - \frac{(\uvec e_z \times i\uvec\Delta_\perp)_\perp }{2M \sqrt{\tau} } \sin\theta \right] G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \qquad - \frac{\uvec\Delta_\perp( \uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec\sigma_\perp) }{2}\left[ \frac{ G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) }{ P^0+M } + \frac{ G_P^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) }{ M } \right]. \end{aligned}\end{equation} We emphasize that the Wigner rotation will in general distort the original distributions in the frame before the Lorentz boost, resulting physical distortions in another frame after the Lorentz boost (\ref{covariant-Lorentz-Transf ), see e.g., the $Q$-dependent $\sin \theta$ and $\cos\theta$ factors in terms of the Wigner rotation angle $\theta$ in $\uvec{\mathcal A _{\text{EF ^\perp$.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-046: \begin{equation}\label{EF-amplitudes-2} \mathcal A_EF^0 = 2 M\gamma \sqrt{1+\tau} \left[ \beta \sigma_z G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \frac{ (\uvec\sigma_\perp \cdot \uvec\Delta_\perp) }{2M} G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \right], \mathcal A_EF^z = 2 M\gamma \sqrt{1+\tau} \left[ \sigma_z G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \beta \frac{ (\uvec\sigma_\perp \cdot \uvec\Delta_\perp) }{2M} G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \right], \uvec{\mathcal A}_{EF}^\perp = 2M \sqrt{1+\tau} \left\lbrace \uvec\sigma_\perp + \frac{(i\uvec\Delta_\perp \times \uvec e_z )_\perp }{|\uvec\Delta_\perp |} \left[ \sin\theta + \frac{( i\uvec\Delta_\perp \times \uvec\sigma)_z }{|\uvec\Delta_\perp|} (\cos\theta-1) \right] \right\rbrace G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \qquad - \frac{ \uvec\Delta_\perp( \uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec\sigma_\perp) }{2M} \left[ \frac{ G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) }{ 1 + \sqrt{1+\tau} } + G_P^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \right], \end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-048: \begin{equation}\label{EF-amplitudes-T2}  \uvec{\mathcal A}_{EF}^\perp = 2\sqrt{P^2} \left[ \uvec\sigma_\perp \cos\theta - \frac{(\uvec e_z \times i\uvec\Delta_\perp)_\perp }{2M \sqrt{\tau} } \sin\theta \right] G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \qquad - \frac{\uvec\Delta_\perp( \uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec\sigma_\perp) }{2}\left[ \frac{ G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) }{ P^0+M } + \frac{ G_P^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) }{ M } \right]. \end{equation}
elements.figure-2411-005: {\includegraphics[angle=0,scale=0.56]{Nucleon_2DEFNCJ0Sz_v3n.pdf}} \caption{Frame-dependence of EF weak-neutral axial charge distributions $J_{5,\text{EF}}^0(b;P_z) $ (upper panel) and $2\pi b \cdot J_{5,\text{EF}}^0(b;P_z) $ (lower panel) as a function $b=|\uvec b_\perp|$ inside a longitudinally polarized (i.e. $\hvec s = \uvec e_z$) proton, using the proton weak-neutral axial FF $G_A^Z(Q^2)$ given in Appendix~\ref{Appendix-A}. Maxima of $2\pi b \cdot J_{5,\text{EF}}^0$ are indicated by the gray dot-dashed curve, and the corresponding root-mean-square transverse axial (charge) radii $\sqrt{\langle b_A^2 \rangle_\text{EF}}$ of $J_{5,\text{EF}}^0$ from Eq.~(\ref{EF-MS-transverse-radii}) are also marked in the lower panel.} \label{Fig_2DEFNC_J0Sz}
elements.sent-2411-136: Applying the general definition (\ref{EF-def ) to the axial-vector four-current operator and inserting the EF amplitudes from Eqs.~(\ref{EF-amplitudes ) or (\ref{EF-amplitudes-2 ,\ref{EF-amplitudes-T2 ), we obtain the following relativistic EF weak-neutral axial-vector four-current distributions: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{2DEF-distributions} J_{5,\text{EF}}^0(\uvec b_\perp;P_z) = \int \frac{\ud^2 \Delta_\perp}{(2\pi)^2}\, e^{-i\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec b_\perp }\, \left[ \left( \frac{P_z}{P^0}\right) \sigma_z G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \frac{ \uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec\sigma_\perp }{2M} G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \right] , J_{5,\text{EF}}^z(\uvec b_\perp;P_z) = \int \frac{\ud^2 \Delta_\perp}{(2\pi)^2}\, e^{-i\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec b_\perp }\, \left[ \sigma_z G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \left( \frac{P_z}{P^0} \right) \frac{ \uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec\sigma_\perp }{2M} G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \right] , \uvec J_{5,\text{EF}}^\perp(\uvec b_\perp;P_z) = \int\frac{\ud^2\Delta_\perp }{(2\pi)^2 }\, e^{-i\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec b_\perp }\, \bigg\{ - \frac{\uvec\Delta_\perp( \uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec\sigma_\perp) }{ 4P^0 }\left[ \frac{ G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) }{ P^0+M } + \frac{ G_P^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) }{ M } \right] + \frac{\sqrt{P^2}}{P^0} \left[ \cos\theta\, \uvec\sigma_\perp - \frac{(\uvec e_z \times i\uvec\Delta_\perp)_\perp }{ 2M\sqrt{\tau} } \sin\theta \right] G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \bigg\}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} In the case $G_T^Z(\uvec \Delta_\perp^2)=0$, we do explicitly reproduce the EF axial-vector four-current distributions given in Ref.~\cite{Chen:2024oxx .}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-049: \begin{equation}\label{2DEF-distributions} J_{5,EF}^0(\uvec b_\perp;P_z) = \int \frac{\ud^2 \Delta_\perp}{(2\pi)^2}\, e^{-i\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec b_\perp }\, \left[ \left( \frac{P_z}{P^0}\right) \sigma_z G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \frac{ \uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec\sigma_\perp }{2M} G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \right] , J_{5,EF}^z(\uvec b_\perp;P_z) = \int \frac{\ud^2 \Delta_\perp}{(2\pi)^2}\, e^{-i\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec b_\perp }\, \left[ \sigma_z G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \left( \frac{P_z}{P^0} \right) \frac{ \uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec\sigma_\perp }{2M} G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \right] , \uvec J_{5,EF}^\perp(\uvec b_\perp;P_z) = \int\frac{\ud^2\Delta_\perp }{(2\pi)^2 }\, e^{-i\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec b_\perp }\, \bigg\{ - \frac{\uvec\Delta_\perp( \uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec\sigma_\perp) }{ 4P^0 }\left[ \frac{ G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) }{ P^0+M } + \frac{ G_P^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) }{ M } \right] + \frac{\sqrt{P^2}}{P^0} \left[ \cos\theta\, \uvec\sigma_\perp - \frac{(\uvec e_z \times i\uvec\Delta_\perp)_\perp }{ 2M\sqrt{\tau} } \sin\theta \right] G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \bigg\}. \end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-142: We observe that the EF distribution $\uvec J_{5,\text{EF ^\perp$ at $P_z = 2~\text{GeV}$, compared with the BF one $\uvec J_{5,B ^\perp$ at $P_z = 0$ in Fig.~\ref{Fig_3DBFNC_Jv , is no longer mirror symmetric with respect to the $x$-axis but is still mirror antisymmetric with respect to the $y$-axis in the transverse plane, as long as $P_z$ is finite and nonvanishing.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-143: The key reason is that on top of the monopole and quadrupole contributions, $\uvec J_{5,\text{EF ^\perp$ at finite $P_z$ also contains a dipole contribution, which explicitly breaks the up-down mirror symmetry (with respect to the $x$-axis) but still preserves the left-right mirror antisymmetry (with respect to the $y$-axis) in the transverse plane for a transversely polarized spin-$\frac{1 {2}$ system.}}}

elements.sent-2411-145: Moreover, we note that both $J_{5,\text{EF ^0$ and $J_{5,\text{EF ^z$ in the generic EF are free from the Wigner rotation (\ref{covariant-Lorentz-Transf ) while $\uvec J_{5,\text{EF ^\perp$ suffers from that (see, e.g., the $\cos\theta$ and $\sin\theta$ factors in $\uvec J_{5,\text{EF ^\perp$), since the Wigner rotation (\ref{covariant-Lorentz-Transf ) mixes $(\uvec\sigma_\perp)_{s's}$, while leaves $(\sigma_z)_{s' s}$ and $(\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec\sigma_\perp)_{s' s}$ unchanged~\cite{Chen:2023dxp .}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-146: Following Refs.~\cite{Chen:2023dxp,Chen:2024oxx , we are now ready to derive mean-square transverse axial and spin radii for a general spin-$\frac{1 {2}$ hadron in the generic EF.}}
elements.sent-2411-147: In the 2D case, the mean-square transverse radius of a 2D spatial distribution $O(\uvec b_\perp;\mathcal K)$ is in general given by \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{2D-MS-radius-def} \langle b_O^2 \rangle(\mathcal K) \equiv \frac{ \int\ud^2 b_\perp\, b^2\, O(\uvec b_\perp;\mathcal K) }{ \int\ud^2 b_\perp\, O(\uvec b_\perp;\mathcal K) }, \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\mathcal K$ denotes a set of external momentum information, e.g., $\mathcal K=P_z$ in the 2D EF case [see e.g. Eq.~(\ref{2DEF-distributions )] and $\mathcal K=P^+$ in the LF case [see e.g. Eq.~(\ref{2DLF-distributions ) in the following Sec.~\ref{sec:Light-front distributions ].}}}
elements.formula-2411-050: \begin{equation}\label{2D-MS-radius-def} \langle b_O^2 \rangle(\mathcal K) \equiv \frac{ \int\ud^2 b_\perp\, b^2\, O(\uvec b_\perp;\mathcal K) }{ \int\ud^2 b_\perp\, O(\uvec b_\perp;\mathcal K) }, \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-148: Applying this general definition (\ref{2D-MS-radius-def ) to the 2D EF axial charge $J_{5,\text{EF ^0(\uvec b_\perp;P_z)$ and longitudinal spin $S_{\text{EF ^z(\uvec b_\perp;P_z)=J_{5,\text{EF ^z(\uvec b_\perp;P_z)/2$ distributions (\ref{2DEF-distributions ), we find \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{EF-MS-transverse-radii} \langle b_A^2 \rangle_\text{EF}(P_z) \equiv \frac{ \int\ud^2 b_\perp\, b^2\, J_{5,\text{EF}}^0(\uvec b_\perp;P_z) }{ \int\ud^2 b_\perp\, J_{5,\text{EF}}^0(\uvec b_\perp;P_z) } = \frac{1}{2E_P^2} + \frac{2}{3} R_A^2, \langle b_{\text{spin},L}^2 \rangle_\text{EF}(P_z) \equiv \frac{ \int\ud^2 b_\perp\, b^2\, S_{\text{EF}}^z(\uvec b_\perp;P_z) }{ \int\ud^2 b_\perp\, S_{\text{EF}}^z(\uvec b_\perp;P_z) } = \frac{2}{3} R_A^2, \end{aligned}\end{equation} which explicitly coincide with the results given in Ref.~\cite{Chen:2024oxx , with $E_P=\sqrt{M^2 + P_z^2}$ and $R_A^2$ given in Eq.~(\ref{naive-3DMS-axial-radius ).}}}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-051: \begin{equation}\label{EF-MS-transverse-radii} \langle b_A^2 \rangle_EF(P_z) \equiv \frac{ \int\ud^2 b_\perp\, b^2\, J_{5,EF}^0(\uvec b_\perp;P_z) }{ \int\ud^2 b_\perp\, J_{5,EF}^0(\uvec b_\perp;P_z) } = \frac{1}{2E_P^2} + \frac{2}{3} R_A^2, \langle b_{spin,L}^2 \rangle_EF(P_z) \equiv \frac{ \int\ud^2 b_\perp\, b^2\, S_{EF}^z(\uvec b_\perp;P_z) }{ \int\ud^2 b_\perp\, S_{EF}^z(\uvec b_\perp;P_z) } = \frac{2}{3} R_A^2, \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-149: Besides, since $\uvec J_{5,\text{EF ^\perp$ in Eq.~(\ref{2DEF-distributions ) assumes exactly the same expression as in Ref.~\cite{Chen:2024oxx , we expect the same result for the mean-square transverse spin radius~\cite{Chen:2024oxx \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{EF-MS-transverse-radii-2} \langle b_{\text{spin},T}^2 \rangle_\text{EF}(P_z) = \frac{2}{3} R_A^2 + \frac{1}{2M^2} \frac{G_P^Z(0)}{G_A^Z(0)} + \frac{1}{2E_P^2} - \frac{1}{2M(E_P+M)}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} In Fig.~\ref{Fig_2DEFNC_MSSpinT , we show the mean-square transverse spin radius $\langle b_{\text{spin ,T ^2 \rangle_\text{EF (P_z)$ as a function of $P_z$ for the proton in the generic EF, where $R_A^2 = 12/(M_A^Z)^2 \approx (0.6510~\text{fm )^2$ and $G_P^Z(0)/G_A^Z(0) = (2M_p/M_\pi)^2 \approx 180.772$ by using the proton weak-neutral axial-vector FFs $G_A^Z(Q^2)$ and $G_P^Z(Q^2)$ given in Appendix~\ref{Appendix-A .}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-150: In particular, we find that the minimum of $\langle b_{\text{spin ,T ^2 \rangle_\text{EF (P_z)$ is located at $P_{z \simeq 3.2132~M_p \approx 3.0149~\text{GeV}$, while the maximum of $\langle b_{\text{spin ,T ^2 \rangle_\text{EF (P_z)$ is located at $P_z =0$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-151: At IMF limit $P_z \to \infty$, the value of $\langle b_{\text{spin ,T ^2 \rangle_\text{EF (\infty)$ is entirely contributed by the first two terms (\ref{EF-MS-transverse-radii-2 ), lying in between $\langle b_{\text{spin ,T ^2 \rangle_\text{EF (0)$ and $\langle b_{\text{spin ,T ^2 \rangle_\text{EF (P_z\simeq 3.2132~M_p)$.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-152: From Eqs.~(\ref{EF-MS-transverse-radii ) and (\ref{EF-MS-transverse-radii-2 ), we conclude that although we explicitly include the second-class current contribution associated with $G_T^Z(Q^2)$ in the full matrix elements (\ref{MatrElem-Axial-Z ) and $G_T^Z(Q^2)$ indeed enters $J_{5,\text{EF ^0$ and $J_{5,\text{EF ^z$ in Eq.~(\ref{2DEF-distributions ), this second-class current contribution does not contribute in fact to the mean-square transverse axial and spin radii in the generic EF.}}}}}}}}
