elements.sent-2411-153: For completeness, we finally study the relativistic axial-vector four-current distributions including the second-class current contribution (\ref{MatrElem-Axial-Z ) by using the LF formalism~\cite{Brodsky:1997de , where LF distributions in some cases can be interpreted as strict probabilistic densities~\cite{Burkardt:2002hr,Miller:2007uy,Carlson:2007xd,Alexandrou:2008bn,Alexandrou:2009hs,Gorchtein:2009qq,Carlson:2009ovh,Miller:2010nz,Miller:2018ybm,Freese:2023jcp,Freese:2023abr , since the symmetry group associated with the transverse LF plane is the Galilean subgroup singled out from the Lorentz group~\cite{Susskind:1967rg,Kogut:1969xa .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-154: In the LF formalism~\cite{Brodsky:1997de , a generic four-vector $a^\mu$ is denoted as $a^\mu=\left[ a^+, a^-, \uvec a_\perp \right]$ with $a^\pm \equiv (a^0 \pm a^3)/\sqrt{2}$.}
elements.sent-2411-155: As a result, scalar products read $p \cdot x = p^\mu x_\mu = p^+ x^- + p^- x^+ - \uvec p_\perp \cdot \uvec x_\perp$, and the constrained LF momentum component $p^-$ is then given by $p^- = (\uvec p_\perp^2+M^2)/(2p^+)$ by using the on-mass-shell condition $p^2=2p^+ p^- - \uvec p_\perp^2=M^2$.
elements.sent-2411-156: It is possible to define $x^+$-independent LF distributions~\cite{Burkardt:2002hr,Miller:2010nz,Lorce:2017wkb in the so-called symmetric LF frame specified by the conditions\footnote{One can relax the condition $\uvec P_\perp=\uvec 0_\perp$, provided that LF distributions are restricted to $x^+=0$~\cite{Freese:2021czn,Freese:2022fat .}}}
elements.sent-2411-157: Similar to Eq.~(\ref{EF-def ), probabilistic LF distributions are in general defined as \begin{equation}\label{LF-def} \begin{aligned} O_\text{LF}(\uvec b_\perp;P^+) \equiv \int \frac{\ud^2 \Delta_\perp}{(2\pi)^2}\, e^{-i\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec b_\perp }\, \frac{ {_\text{LF}}\langle p',\lambda'| \hat{O}(0)|p,\lambda\rangle_\text{LF} }{2P^+} \bigg|_{\Delta^+=|\uvec P_\perp|=0}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $P^+$ is treated as an independent variable in LF formalism, and LF helicity states $|p,\lambda\rangle_\text{LF}$ are covariantly normalized as \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} {_\text{LF}}\langle p',\lambda'|p,\lambda\rangle_\text{LF} = 2p^+ (2\pi)^3 \delta(p^{\prime\, +} - p^+) \delta^{(2)}( \uvec p_\perp^\prime -\uvec p_\perp ) \delta_{\lambda' \lambda}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} and can be related to the generic canonical spin states $|p, s \rangle$ via the Melosh rotation~\cite{Melosh:1974cu \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{Melosh-rotation} |p,\lambda \rangle_\text{LF} = \sum_s \frac{ |p, s \rangle\langle p,s| }{ \langle p,s|p,s\rangle } |p,\lambda\rangle_\text{LF} = \sum_s |p,s \rangle \mathcal M_{s\lambda}^{(j)}(p),\qquad \mathcal M_{s\lambda}^{(j)}(p)\equiv \frac{ \langle p,s|p,\lambda\rangle_\text{LF} }{ \langle p,s|p,s\rangle }, \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\mathcal M^{(j) _{s\lambda (p)$ denotes the unitary matrix elements of the Melosh rotation for a spin-$j$ system.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-053: \begin{equation}\label{LF-def}  O_LF(\uvec b_\perp;P^+) \equiv \int \frac{\ud^2 \Delta_\perp}{(2\pi)^2}\, e^{-i\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec b_\perp }\, \frac{ {_LF}\langle p',\lambda'| \hat{O}(0)|p,\lambda\rangle_LF }{2P^+} \bigg|_{\Delta^+=|\uvec P_\perp|=0}, \end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-055: \begin{equation}\label{Melosh-rotation} |p,\lambda \rangle_LF = \sum_s \frac{ |p, s \rangle\langle p,s| }{ \langle p,s|p,s\rangle } |p,\lambda\rangle_LF = \sum_s |p,s \rangle \mathcal M_{s\lambda}^{(j)}(p),\qquad \mathcal M_{s\lambda}^{(j)}(p)\equiv \frac{ \langle p,s|p,\lambda\rangle_LF }{ \langle p,s|p,s\rangle }, \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-158: We note that some of the unusual distortions appearing in LF distributions are relativistic artefacts caused by Melosh rotations~\cite{Lorce:2011zta,Lorce:2020onh,Lorce:2022jyi,Chen:2022smg,Chen:2023dxp , since $\mathcal M^{(j) (p)$ in general is not an identity matrix.}}
elements.sent-2411-159: In the spin-$\frac{1 {2}$ case that we mainly focus on in this work, the generic $2\times 2$ unitary Melosh rotation matrix $\mathcal M^{(1/2)}$ is explicitly given by~\cite{Lorce:2011zta,Chen:2022smg,Chen:2023dxp \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{Melosh-rotation-M} \mathcal M^{(1/2)}(p) = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\frac{\theta_M}{2} -e^{-i\phi_p}\sin\frac{\theta_M}{2} e^{i\phi_p } \sin\frac{\theta_M}{2} \cos\frac{\theta_M }{2} \end{pmatrix} = \frac{ 1 }{ \sqrt{ \mathcal N_p } } \begin{pmatrix} p^0 + p_z + M, -(p_x-i p_y) p_x+i p_y, p^0 + p_z + M \end{pmatrix}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} with \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{Melosh-angles} \cos\theta_M = \frac{ (p^0 + p_z + M)^2 - |\uvec p_\perp|^2 }{ \mathcal N_p }, \sin\theta_M = -\frac{ 2(p^0 + p_z + M) |\uvec p_\perp| }{ \mathcal N_p }, \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\mathcal N_p \equiv 2(p^0+p_z)(p^0+M) = (p^0+p_z+m)^2 + \uvec p_\perp^2$, $\uvec p_\perp = |\uvec p_\perp|(\cos\phi_p, \sin\phi_p)$, and $\theta_M$ is the Melosh rotation angle.}}
elements.formula-2411-056: \begin{equation}\label{Melosh-rotation-M} \mathcal M^{(1/2)}(p) =  \cos\frac{\theta_M}{2} -e^{-i\phi_p}\sin\frac{\theta_M}{2} e^{i\phi_p } \sin\frac{\theta_M}{2} \cos\frac{\theta_M }{2}  = \frac{ 1 }{ \sqrt{ \mathcal N_p } }  p^0 + p_z + M, -(p_x-i p_y) p_x+i p_y, p^0 + p_z + M , \end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-057: \begin{equation}\label{Melosh-angles} \cos\theta_M = \frac{ (p^0 + p_z + M)^2 - |\uvec p_\perp|^2 }{ \mathcal N_p }, \sin\theta_M = -\frac{ 2(p^0 + p_z + M) |\uvec p_\perp| }{ \mathcal N_p }, \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-160: It is easily to verify that $\cos^2\theta_M + \sin^2\theta_M=1$ is indeed automatically guaranteed.
elements.figure-2411-009: {\includegraphics[angle=0,scale=1.05]{Melosh_rotation_angle_v3n.pdf}} \caption{Melosh rotation angle $\theta_M$ (left panel) and $\cos\theta_M$ (right panel) for the proton in the generic EF as a function of the average momentum $P_z$ and the momentum transfer magnitude $Q=|\uvec\Delta_\perp|$.} \label{Fig_2DLFNC_MeloshAngle}
elements.sent-2411-161: In Fig.~\ref{Fig_2DLFNC_MeloshAngle , we illustrate the Melosh rotation angle $\theta_M$ and $\cos\theta_M$ for the proton in the generic EF as a function of the average momentum $P_z=p_z$ and the momentum transfer magnitude $Q=|\uvec\Delta_\perp|=2|\uvec p_\perp|$.}
elements.sent-2411-162: In particular when $P_z = 0$, we have \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \lim_{P_z \to 0}\, \cos\theta_M = \frac{1}{ \sqrt{1+\tau} },\qquad \lim_{P_z \to 0}\, \sin\theta_M = -\frac{ \sqrt{\tau} }{ \sqrt{1+\tau} }, \end{aligned}\end{equation} which are well consistent with what we have found in Ref.~\cite{Chen:2022smg .}
elements.sent-2411-163: When $P_z \to \infty$, $\theta_M = 0$ and $\mathcal M^{(1/2) (p)$ will be an identity matrix.}
elements.sent-2411-164: We also observe that as long as $Q \neq 0$ and $|P_z| \neq \infty$, $\mathcal M^{(1/2) (p)$ will not be an identity matrix.}
elements.sent-2411-165: In most of the LF distributions in the literature, see e.g. Refs.~\cite{Burkardt:2002hr,Miller:2007uy,Carlson:2007xd,Alexandrou:2008bn,Alexandrou:2009hs,Gorchtein:2009qq,Carlson:2009ovh,Miller:2010nz,Miller:2018ybm , $Q = 2|\uvec p_\perp| \neq 0$ is evident since people wish to present the 2D LF distributions on the transverse (or impact parameter) plane.}
elements.sent-2411-166: One would argue that the as long as the limit $P_z \to \infty$ is taken, LF amplitudes should be completely free from relativistic artefacts caused by Melosh rotations, since the LF amplitudes can be explicitly obtained via the IMF limit $P_z \to \infty$ of the EF amplitudes~\cite{Chen:2022smg,Chen:2023dxp,Chen:2024oxx .}
elements.sent-2411-167: This is not true, since when we take the IMF limit $P_z \to \infty$ of EF amplitudes, see e.g. Sec.~\ref{LF: LF amplitudes via proper IMF limit of EF amplitudes , we keep both $(P^0 + P_z)/\sqrt{2}\equiv P^+$ and $ (P^0 - P_z)/\sqrt{2}\equiv P^-$ fixed.}
elements.sent-2411-168: When $P^+ \neq \infty$ and $P^- \neq 0$, relativistic artefacts caused by Melosh rotations may have been inherited partially in the LF amplitudes, see e.g. $\mathcal A_\text{LF ^-$ in Eq.~(\ref{LF-amplitudes ).}}
elements.sent-2411-169: Typically, when a well-defined LF distribution is completely independent of both $P^+$ and $P^-$, e.g. the LF electric charge distribution $J_\text{LF ^+$~\cite{Chen:2023dxp and axial charge distribution $J_{5,\text{LF ^+$ (\ref{2DLF-distributions ) of the nucleon, it should be free from relativistic artefacts caused by Melosh rotations~\cite{Lorce:2011zta,Lorce:2020onh,Lorce:2022jyi,Chen:2022smg,Chen:2023dxp .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-170: From the Melosh rotation perspective, this also explains the reason why the LF components $\hat j^+$ and $\hat j^-$ are usually regarded as the ``good" and ``bad" components in the literature~\cite{Chen:2022smg , respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-171: In this sense, we believe that the pictures provided by those LF densities that depend explicitly on $P^+$ or $P^-$ can not be considered as realistic representations of the system (on the average) at rest~\cite{Chen:2023dxp .}
elements.sent-2411-172: Another substantial part of distortions appearing in LF distributions are physically induced by Wigner rotations~\cite{Chen:2022smg .}
elements.sent-2411-173: According to the generic Lorentz transformation~\cite{Jacob:1959at,Durand:1962zza of matrix elements from one frame $\Sigma$ to another Lorentz frame $\Sigma'$ based on Poincar\'e symmetry, the Wigner rotation will in general distort the distributions (in the $\Sigma$ frame) during the Lorentz boost, resulting therefore physical distortions in the $\Sigma'$ frame.}
elements.sent-2411-174: Since one can always explicitly reproduce the LF amplitudes based on the proper IMF limit of canonical EF amplitudes~\cite{Chen:2022smg,Chen:2023dxp,Chen:2024oxx where the Wigner rotation has already played an important role, see e.g. Sec.~\ref{LF: LF amplitudes via proper IMF limit of EF amplitudes , we conclude that some of the distortions appearing in LF distributions are natural relativistic consequences of physical Wigner rotations rather than artefacts owing to the change of Dirac spinors from canonical spin basis to LF helicity basis (\ref{Melosh-rotation ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-175: By analogy with Eq.~(\ref{MatrElem-Axial-Z ), matrix elements of the axial-vector four-current operator in terms of LF helicity states $|p,\lambda \rangle_\text{LF}$ for a general spin-$\frac{1 {2}$ system are parametrized as~\cite{Chen:2024oxx \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{MatrElem-Axial-Z-LF} {_\text{LF}}\langle p',\lambda'| \hat{j}_5^\mu(0)|p,\lambda\rangle_\text{LF} = \bar{u}_\text{LF}(p',\lambda')\left[ \gamma^\mu G_A^Z + \frac{\Delta^{\mu}}{2 M}G_P^Z + \frac{i\sigma^{\mu\nu}\Delta_\nu }{2M} G_T^Z \right] \gamma^5 u_\text{LF}(p,\lambda), \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $u_\text{LF (p,\lambda)$ denotes the LF helicity Dirac spinor, and explicit $Q^2$-dependence of these axial-vector FFs $G_A^Z(Q^2)$, $G_P^Z(Q^2)$, and $G_T^Z(Q^2)$ for clarity is omitted.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-176: Evaluating directly the matrix elements (\ref{MatrElem-Axial-Z-LF ) in the symmetric LF frame leads to~\cite{Chen:2024oxx \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{LF-amplitudes} \mathcal A_\text{LF}^+ = 2P^+ (\sigma_z)_{\lambda' \lambda} G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \frac{P^+}{M} (\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec\sigma_\perp)_{\lambda' \lambda} G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2), \mathcal A_\text{LF}^- = -2P^- (\sigma_z)_{\lambda' \lambda} G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \frac{P^-}{M} (\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec\sigma_\perp)_{\lambda' \lambda} G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2), \uvec{\mathcal A}_{\text{EF}}^\perp = \left[ 2M(\uvec\sigma_\perp)_{\lambda' \lambda} + (\uvec e_z \times i\uvec\Delta_\perp)_\perp \delta_{ \lambda' \lambda } \right]G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) - \frac{\uvec\Delta_\perp \left(\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec\sigma_\perp \right)_{\lambda'\lambda} }{2M} G_P^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2), \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\mathcal A_\text{LF ^\mu \equiv {_\text{LF }\langle p',\lambda'|\hat j_{5 ^{\mu (0)|p,\lambda\rangle_\text{LF}$, $p'=[P^+, P^-, \uvec\Delta_\perp/2]$, $p=[P^+, P^-, -\uvec\Delta_\perp/2]$, $Q^2=\uvec\Delta_\perp^2$, $P^2=M^2(1+\tau)$ and $P^- = M^2(1+\tau)/(2P^+)$.}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-060: \begin{equation}\label{LF-amplitudes} \mathcal A_LF^+ = 2P^+ (\sigma_z)_{\lambda' \lambda} G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \frac{P^+}{M} (\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec\sigma_\perp)_{\lambda' \lambda} G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2), \mathcal A_LF^- = -2P^- (\sigma_z)_{\lambda' \lambda} G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \frac{P^-}{M} (\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec\sigma_\perp)_{\lambda' \lambda} G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2), \uvec{\mathcal A}_{EF}^\perp = \left[ 2M(\uvec\sigma_\perp)_{\lambda' \lambda} + (\uvec e_z \times i\uvec\Delta_\perp)_\perp \delta_{ \lambda' \lambda } \right]G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) - \frac{\uvec\Delta_\perp \left(\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec\sigma_\perp \right)_{\lambda'\lambda} }{2M} G_P^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2), \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-177: Applying the general definition (\ref{LF-def ) to the axial-vector four-current operator and inserting the EF amplitudes (\ref{LF-amplitudes ), we obtain the following LF weak-neutral axial-vector four-current distributions: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{2DLF-distributions} J_{5,\text{LF}}^+(\uvec b_\perp;P^+) = \int \frac{\ud^2 \Delta_\perp}{(2\pi)^2}\, e^{-i\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec b_\perp }\, \left[ (\sigma_z)_{\lambda' \lambda} G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \frac{ (\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec\sigma_\perp)_{\lambda' \lambda} }{2M} G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \right] , J_{5,\text{LF}}^-(\uvec b_\perp;P^+) = \int \frac{\ud^2 \Delta_\perp}{(2\pi)^2}\, e^{-i\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec b_\perp }\, \frac{P^-}{P^+} \left[ -(\sigma_z)_{\lambda' \lambda} G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \frac{ (\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec\sigma_\perp)_{\lambda' \lambda} }{2M} G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \right] , \uvec J_{5,\text{LF}}^\perp(\uvec b_\perp;P^+) = \int\frac{\ud^2\Delta_\perp }{(2\pi)^2 }\, e^{-i\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec b_\perp }\, \frac{M}{P^+} \bigg\{ \left[ (\uvec\sigma_\perp)_{\lambda' \lambda} + \frac{(\uvec e_z \times i\uvec\Delta_\perp)_\perp }{2M} \delta_{ \lambda' \lambda } \right]G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \qquad - \frac{\uvec\Delta_\perp \left(\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec\sigma_\perp \right)_{\lambda'\lambda} }{ 4M^2 } G_P^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \bigg\}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} In the case $G_T^Z(\uvec \Delta_\perp^2)=0$, we do explicitly reproduce the LF axial-vector four-current distributions given in Ref.~\cite{Chen:2024oxx .}}}
elements.formula-2411-061: \begin{equation}\label{2DLF-distributions} J_{5,LF}^+(\uvec b_\perp;P^+) = \int \frac{\ud^2 \Delta_\perp}{(2\pi)^2}\, e^{-i\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec b_\perp }\, \left[ (\sigma_z)_{\lambda' \lambda} G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \frac{ (\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec\sigma_\perp)_{\lambda' \lambda} }{2M} G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \right] , J_{5,LF}^-(\uvec b_\perp;P^+) = \int \frac{\ud^2 \Delta_\perp}{(2\pi)^2}\, e^{-i\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec b_\perp }\, \frac{P^-}{P^+} \left[ -(\sigma_z)_{\lambda' \lambda} G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \frac{ (\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec\sigma_\perp)_{\lambda' \lambda} }{2M} G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \right] , \uvec J_{5,LF}^\perp(\uvec b_\perp;P^+) = \int\frac{\ud^2\Delta_\perp }{(2\pi)^2 }\, e^{-i\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec b_\perp }\, \frac{M}{P^+} \bigg\{ \left[ (\uvec\sigma_\perp)_{\lambda' \lambda} + \frac{(\uvec e_z \times i\uvec\Delta_\perp)_\perp }{2M} \delta_{ \lambda' \lambda } \right]G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \qquad - \frac{\uvec\Delta_\perp \left(\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec\sigma_\perp \right)_{\lambda'\lambda} }{ 4M^2 } G_P^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \bigg\}. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-178: Again, we confirm that the transverse LF axial-vector current $\uvec J_{5,\text{LF ^\perp$~(\ref{2DLF-distributions ) is independent of the second-class current contribution associated with $G_T^Z$.}}}
elements.figure-2411-010: {\includegraphics[angle=0,scale=0.55]{Nucleon_2DLFNCJvT_v3n.pdf}} \caption{The (scaled) transverse LF weak-neutral axial-vector current distribution $\tfrac{P^+}{M}\uvec J_{5,\text{LF}}^\perp(\uvec b_\perp;P^+)$ in the transverse plane inside a transversely polarized (i.e. $\hvec s = \uvec e_x$) proton, using the proton weak-neutral axial-vector FFs $G_A^Z(Q^2)$ and $G_P^Z(Q^2)$ given in Appendix~\ref{Appendix-A}.} \label{Fig_3DLFNC_JvT}
elements.sent-2411-179: According to Eqs.~(\ref{2DLF-distributions ) and (\ref{2DEF-distributions ), one can explicitly show that \begin{equation}\label{EF-IMF-LF-equal} J_{5,\text{LF}}^+(\uvec b_\perp;P^+) = J_{5,\text{EF}}^0(\uvec b_\perp;\infty) = J_{5,\text{EF}}^z(\uvec b_\perp;\infty),\end{equation} where the canonical spin polarizations coincide with the LF helicities in the IMF limit $P_z \to \infty$ (see Sec.~\ref{LF: LF amplitudes via proper IMF limit of EF amplitudes ).}}}
elements.formula-2411-062: \begin{equation}\label{EF-IMF-LF-equal} J_{5,LF}^+(\uvec b_\perp;P^+) = J_{5,EF}^0(\uvec b_\perp;\infty) = J_{5,EF}^z(\uvec b_\perp;\infty),\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-180: Above result (\ref{EF-IMF-LF-equal ) is quite reminiscent of what we have found in the electromagnetic four-current~\cite{Chen:2022smg and polarization-magnetization~\cite{Chen:2023dxp cases.}}}
elements.sent-2411-181: It is therefore clear that for a longitudinally polarized proton, $J_{5,\text{LF ^+(b;P^+)$ and $2\pi b \cdot J_{5,\text{LF ^+(b;P^+)$ will be exactly the same as the $P_z = \infty$ case of $J_{5,\text{EF ^0(b;P_z) $ and $2\pi b \cdot J_{5,\text{EF ^0(b;P_z) $ in Fig.~\ref{Fig_2DEFNC_J0Sz , respectively.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-182: Similarly for a transversely polarized proton, $iJ_{5,\text{LF ^+(b;P^+)$ and $2\pi b \cdot iJ_{5,\text{LF ^+(b;P^+)$ will be exactly the same as the $P_z = \infty$ case of $iJ_{5,\text{EF ^z(\uvec b_\perp;P_z) $ and $2\pi b \cdot iJ_{5,\text{EF ^z(\uvec b_\perp;P_z) $ in Fig.~\ref{Fig_2DEFNC_JzSx , respectively.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-183: In Fig.~\ref{Fig_3DLFNC_JvT , we illustrate the (scaled) transverse LF axial-vector current distribution $\tfrac{P^+ {M}\uvec J_{5,\text{LF ^\perp(\uvec b_\perp;P^+)$ in the transverse plane inside a transversely polarized proton, using the proton weak-neutral axial-vector FFs $G_A^Z(Q^2)$ and $G_P^Z(Q^2)$ given in Appendix~\ref{Appendix-A .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-184: Similarly to Fig.~\ref{Fig_3DEFNC_JvT , the distribution $\tfrac{P^+ {M}\uvec J_{5,\text{LF ^\perp$ in the transverse plane is not mirror symmetric with respect to the $x$-axis but it is still mirror antisymmetric with respect to the $y$-axis, since $\uvec J_{5,\text{LF ^\perp$ also contains monopole, dipole and quadrupole contributions similar as $\uvec J_{5,\text{EF ^\perp$.}}}}}}}}
elements.subsection-2411-009: LF amplitudes via proper IMF limit of EF amplitudes
elements.sent-2411-185: Following Ref.~\cite{Chen:2022smg , we can explicitly reproduce the LF amplitudes (\ref{LF-amplitudes ) by making use of the EF amplitudes (\ref{EF-amplitudes ) in the proper IMF limit (i.e. first keep $P^+$ and $P^-$ fixed, and then take $P_z \to \infty$ limit).}}}
elements.sent-2411-186: Starting from Eq.~(\ref{EF-amplitudes ), we can first construct the following $\mathcal A^+_\text{EF}$ and $\mathcal A^-_\text{EF}$ amplitudes in a generic EF: \begin{equation}\label{construced-EF-amplitudes} \begin{aligned} \langle p',s'| \hat j_5^+(0) |p, s\rangle = \frac{ \mathcal A_\text{EF}^0 + \mathcal A_\text{EF}^z }{ \sqrt{2} } = 2P^+ \left[ (\sigma_z)_{s's} G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \frac{(\uvec\sigma_\perp \cdot \uvec\Delta_\perp)_{s's}}{2M} G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \right], \langle p',s'| \hat j_5^+(0) |p, s\rangle = \frac{ \mathcal A_\text{EF}^0 - \mathcal A_\text{EF}^z }{ \sqrt{2} } = 2P^-\left[ -(\sigma_z)_{s' s} G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \frac{ (\uvec\sigma_\perp \cdot \uvec\Delta_\perp)_{s' s} }{2M} G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \right], \end{aligned}\end{equation} with $\hat j_5^\pm \equiv (\hat j_5^0 \pm \hat j_5^z)/\sqrt{2}$.}
elements.formula-2411-063: \begin{equation}\label{construced-EF-amplitudes}  \langle p',s'| \hat j_5^+(0) |p, s\rangle = \frac{ \mathcal A_EF^0 + \mathcal A_EF^z }{ \sqrt{2} } = 2P^+ \left[ (\sigma_z)_{s's} G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \frac{(\uvec\sigma_\perp \cdot \uvec\Delta_\perp)_{s's}}{2M} G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \right], \langle p',s'| \hat j_5^+(0) |p, s\rangle = \frac{ \mathcal A_EF^0 - \mathcal A_EF^z }{ \sqrt{2} } = 2P^-\left[ -(\sigma_z)_{s' s} G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \frac{ (\uvec\sigma_\perp \cdot \uvec\Delta_\perp)_{s' s} }{2M} G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \right], \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-187: We note that $\langle p',s'| \hat{\uvec j _5^\perp(0) |p, s\rangle = \uvec{\mathcal A _{\text{EF ^\perp$ remains the same as in Eq.~(\ref{EF-amplitudes ).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-188: Above amplitudes $\langle p',s'| \hat j_5^+(0) |p, s\rangle$ and $\langle p',s'| \hat j_5^+(0) |p, s\rangle$ are clearly not proper LF amplitudes since they are still defined in terms of the canonical (or instant-form) polarization states $|s \rangle$ rather than the LF helicity states $|\lambda \rangle_\text{LF}$~\cite{Chen:2022smg .}
elements.sent-2411-189: By keeping $P^+$ and $P^-$ fixed, we then take the $P_z \to \infty$ limit of $\langle p',s'| \hat j_5^+(0) |p, s\rangle$, $\langle p',s'| \hat j_5^-(0) |p, s\rangle$ and $\langle p',s'| \hat{\uvec j _5^\perp(0) |p, s\rangle$.}
elements.sent-2411-190: It then follows that \begin{equation}\label{construced-IMF-amplitudes} \begin{aligned} \lim_{P_z \to \infty}\, \langle p',s'| \hat j_5^+(0) |p, s\rangle \Big|_{P^\pm} = 2P^+ \left[ (\sigma_z)_{s's} G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \frac{(\uvec\sigma_\perp \cdot \uvec\Delta_\perp)_{s's}}{2M} G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \right], \lim_{P_z \to \infty}\, \langle p',s'| \hat j_5^-(0) |p, s\rangle\Big|_{P^\pm} = P^-\left[ -(\sigma_z)_{s' s} G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \frac{ (\uvec\sigma_\perp \cdot \uvec\Delta_\perp)_{s' s} }{2M} G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \right], \lim_{P_z \to \infty}\, \langle p',s'| \hat{\uvec j}_5^\perp(0) |p, s\rangle\Big|_{P^\pm} = 2M\left[ (\uvec\sigma_\perp)_{s' s} + \frac{(\uvec e_z \times i\uvec\Delta_\perp)_\perp }{2M} \delta_{s' s} \right]G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \quad - \frac{\uvec\Delta_\perp \left(\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec\sigma_\perp \right)_{s' s} }{2M} G_P^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2), \end{aligned}\end{equation} We note that in the IMF limit (i.e., $P_z \to \infty$), similarly to the electromagnetic case~\cite{Chen:2022smg , the amplitude $\langle p',s'| \hat j_5^+(0) |p, s\rangle$ will be enhanced while the amplitude $\langle p',s'| \hat j_5^-(0) |p, s\rangle$ will be suppressed, owing to the associated global factors $P^+$ and $P^-$, respectively.}
elements.formula-2411-064: \begin{equation}\label{construced-IMF-amplitudes}  \lim_{P_z \to \infty}\, \langle p',s'| \hat j_5^+(0) |p, s\rangle \Big|_{P^\pm} = 2P^+ \left[ (\sigma_z)_{s's} G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \frac{(\uvec\sigma_\perp \cdot \uvec\Delta_\perp)_{s's}}{2M} G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \right], \lim_{P_z \to \infty}\, \langle p',s'| \hat j_5^-(0) |p, s\rangle\Big|_{P^\pm} = P^-\left[ -(\sigma_z)_{s' s} G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \frac{ (\uvec\sigma_\perp \cdot \uvec\Delta_\perp)_{s' s} }{2M} G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \right], \lim_{P_z \to \infty}\, \langle p',s'| \hat{\uvec j}_5^\perp(0) |p, s\rangle\Big|_{P^\pm} = 2M\left[ (\uvec\sigma_\perp)_{s' s} + \frac{(\uvec e_z \times i\uvec\Delta_\perp)_\perp }{2M} \delta_{s' s} \right]G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \quad - \frac{\uvec\Delta_\perp \left(\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec\sigma_\perp \right)_{s' s} }{2M} G_P^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2), \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-191: Compared with Eq.~(\ref{LF-amplitudes ), we see that above ``constructed" EF amplitudes (\ref{construced-IMF-amplitudes ) in the proper IMF limit almost coincides the genuine LF amplitudes (\ref{LF-amplitudes ), except the polarization difference.}}}
elements.sent-2411-192: Recall the fact that canonical polarization states $|s \rangle$ in the $P_z \to \infty$ limit (or equivalently, $M \to 0$ and $Q=|\uvec\Delta_\perp| \to 0$ limits) will coincide with the LF helicity states $|\lambda \rangle_\text{LF}$, namely $\lim_{P_z \to \infty |s \rangle = |\lambda \rangle_\text{LF}$ since $\mathcal M^{(1/2) (p)=\mathcal M^{\dag (1/2) (p') = \mathds1_{2\times 2}$ at $P_z \to \infty$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-193: In other words, we can directly make the following replacements \begin{equation}\label{s-replacements} \begin{aligned} s \quad \to\quad \lambda,\qquad s' \quad \to\quad \lambda' \end{aligned}\end{equation} for the ``constructed" amplitudes (\ref{construced-IMF-amplitudes ) at proper IMF limit.}
elements.sent-2411-194: It then follows that \begin{equation}\label{construced-LF-amplitudes} \begin{aligned} \lim_{P_z \to \infty}\, \langle p',s'| \hat j_5^+(0) |p, s\rangle \Big|_{P^\pm,~s\to \lambda,~s' \to \lambda'} = 2P^+ \left[ (\sigma_z)_{\lambda' \lambda} G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \frac{ (\uvec\sigma_\perp \cdot \uvec\Delta_\perp)_{\lambda' \lambda} }{2M} G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \right] = \mathcal A_\text{LF}^+, \lim_{P_z \to \infty}\, \langle p',s'| \hat j_5^+(0) |p, s\rangle\Big|_{P^\pm,~s\to \lambda,~s' \to \lambda'} = 2P^-\left[ -(\sigma_z)_{\lambda' \lambda} G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \frac{ (\uvec\sigma_\perp \cdot \uvec\Delta_\perp)_{\lambda' \lambda} }{2M} G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \right] = \mathcal A_\text{LF}^-, \lim_{P_z \to \infty}\, \langle p',s'| \hat{\uvec j}_5^\perp(0) |p, s\rangle\Big|_{P^\pm,~s\to \lambda,~s' \to \lambda'} = 2M\left[ (\uvec\sigma_\perp)_{\lambda' \lambda} + \frac{(\uvec e_z \times i\uvec\Delta_\perp)_\perp }{2M } \delta_{\lambda' \lambda} \right] G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) - \frac{\uvec\Delta_\perp \left(\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec\sigma_\perp \right)_{\lambda' \lambda} }{2M} G_P^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) = \uvec{\mathcal A}_\text{LF}^\perp, \end{aligned}\end{equation} which indeed explicitly coincide with the genuine LF amplitudes (\ref{LF-amplitudes ) as expected~\cite{Chen:2022smg .}}
elements.formula-2411-066: \begin{equation}\label{construced-LF-amplitudes}  \lim_{P_z \to \infty}\, \langle p',s'| \hat j_5^+(0) |p, s\rangle \Big|_{P^\pm,~s\to \lambda,~s' \to \lambda'} = 2P^+ \left[ (\sigma_z)_{\lambda' \lambda} G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \frac{ (\uvec\sigma_\perp \cdot \uvec\Delta_\perp)_{\lambda' \lambda} }{2M} G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \right] = \mathcal A_LF^+, \lim_{P_z \to \infty}\, \langle p',s'| \hat j_5^+(0) |p, s\rangle\Big|_{P^\pm,~s\to \lambda,~s' \to \lambda'} = 2P^-\left[ -(\sigma_z)_{\lambda' \lambda} G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \frac{ (\uvec\sigma_\perp \cdot \uvec\Delta_\perp)_{\lambda' \lambda} }{2M} G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \right] = \mathcal A_LF^-, \lim_{P_z \to \infty}\, \langle p',s'| \hat{\uvec j}_5^\perp(0) |p, s\rangle\Big|_{P^\pm,~s\to \lambda,~s' \to \lambda'} = 2M\left[ (\uvec\sigma_\perp)_{\lambda' \lambda} + \frac{(\uvec e_z \times i\uvec\Delta_\perp)_\perp }{2M } \delta_{\lambda' \lambda} \right] G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) - \frac{\uvec\Delta_\perp \left(\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec\sigma_\perp \right)_{\lambda' \lambda} }{2M} G_P^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) = \uvec{\mathcal A}_LF^\perp, \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-195: Alternatively, we can also explicitly reproduce the LF amplitudes (\ref{LF-amplitudes ) by using the EF amplitudes in Eqs.~(\ref{EF-amplitudes-2 ,\ref{EF-amplitudes-T2 ) obtained via the covariant Lorentz transformation (\ref{covariant-Lorentz-Transf ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-196: Similarly to Eq.~(\ref{construced-EF-amplitudes ), we can construct $\langle p',s'| \hat j_5^+(0) |p, s\rangle$ and $\langle p',s'| \hat j_5^-(0) |p, s\rangle$ from Eq.~(\ref{EF-amplitudes-2 ) as follows \begin{equation}\label{construced-EF-amplitudes-2} \begin{aligned} \langle p',s'| \hat j_5^+(0) |p, s\rangle = \sqrt{2}(1+\beta) \gamma M \sqrt{1+\tau} \left[ \sigma_z G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \frac{ (\uvec\sigma_\perp \cdot \uvec\Delta_\perp) }{2M} G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \right], = 2P^+ \left[ \sigma_z G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \frac{ (\uvec\sigma_\perp \cdot \uvec\Delta_\perp) }{2M} G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \right], \langle p',s'| \hat j_5^+(0) |p, s\rangle = \sqrt{2}(1-\beta) \gamma M \sqrt{1+\tau} \left[ -\sigma_z G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \frac{ (\uvec\sigma_\perp \cdot \uvec\Delta_\perp) }{2M} G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \right], = 2P^- \left[ -\sigma_z G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \frac{ (\uvec\sigma_\perp \cdot \uvec\Delta_\perp) }{2M} G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \right], \end{aligned}\end{equation} where we have used Eq.~(\ref{gamma-beta ).}}}

elements.sent-2411-198: By taking then the proper IMF limit of $\langle p',s'| \hat j_5^+(0) |p, s\rangle$, $\langle p',s'| \hat j_5^-(0) |p, s\rangle$ and $\langle p',s'| \hat{\uvec j _5^\perp(0) |p, s\rangle$, we do reproduce Eq.~(\ref{construced-IMF-amplitudes ) with the help that~\cite{Chen:2022smg}\footnote{We note that there is a typo in the Eq.~(41) of Ref.~\cite{Chen:2022smg that ``$\lim_{P_z \to \infty}\, \tan\theta = -1/\sqrt{\tau}$" should be corrected as ``$\lim_{P_z \to \infty}\, \tan\theta = -\sqrt{\tau}$", see Eq.~(\ref{Wigner-angles-IMF ).. \begin{equation}\label{Wigner-angles-IMF} \lim_{P_z \to \infty }\, \cos\theta = \frac{1}{ \sqrt{1+\tau} },\qquad \lim_{P_z \to \infty }\, \sin\theta = -\frac{ \sqrt{\tau} }{ \sqrt{1+\tau} }.\end{equation}.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-202: Any light-front (LF) amplitudes for well-defined LF distributions in principle can be explicitly reproduced from the corresponding elastic frame (EF) amplitudes in the proper infinite-momentum frame (IMF) limit (i.e., first keep $P^+$ and $P^-$ fixed and then take $P_z \to \infty$).
elements.sent-2411-203: The first key source of distributions is at amplitude level, originating from complicated Wigner rotations and the mixing of temporal and longitudinal components under longitudinal Lorentz boosts according to the covariant Lorentz transformation (\ref{covariant-Lorentz-Transf ) for amplitudes from BF to another Lorentz frame (e.g., a generic EF).}
elements.sent-2411-204: This is the only source of physical distortions for EF distributions.
elements.sent-2411-205: The second source of distortions in LF distributions is also at amplitude level, originating from relativistic artefacts caused by Melosh rotations (i.e. the change of Dirac spinors from canonical spin basis to LF helicity basis), see Eq.~(\ref{Melosh-rotation ).}
elements.sent-2411-206: The change of polarization basis in general affects also the normalization of the amplitudes, which usually brings the $P^+$-dependence for the transverse components of LF distributions, see e.g. the transverse LF axial-vector current distribution $\uvec J_{5,\text{LF ^\perp(\uvec b_\perp;P^+)$ in Eq.~(\ref{2DLF-distributions ) and Fig.~\ref{Fig_3DLFNC_JvT .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-207: The third source of distortions in LF distributions is due to the peculiar LF perspective for defining the LF $\hat O^+$ and $\hat O^-$ components~\cite{Chen:2022smg,Chen:2023dxp .}
elements.sent-2411-208: The $\hat O^+$ component is usually regarded as the ``good" component, allowing physically clear probabilistic interpretation~\cite{Soper:1976jc,Burkardt:2002hr,Miller:2010nz .}
elements.sent-2411-209: The $\hat O^-$ component, however, is usually regarded as the ``bad" component, and it is also considered as a complicated object without clear physical interpretation~\cite{Chen:2022smg and is therefore often just ignored in the literature.}
elements.sent-2411-210: Similarly to Eq.~(\ref{EF-MS-transverse-radii ), we can also apply the general definition (\ref{2D-MS-radius-def ) to the 2D LF axial charge $J_{5,\text{LF ^+(\uvec b_\perp;P^+)$ and longitudinal spin $S_{L,\text{LF (\uvec b_\perp;P^+) \equiv \frac{1 {4 \text{Tr}\left[ \sigma_z J_{5,\text{LF ^+(\uvec b_\perp;P^+) \right]$ distributions (\ref{2DLF-distributions ).}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-211: It then follows that \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{LF-MS-transverse-radii} \langle b_A^2 \rangle_\text{LF}(P^+) = \langle b_{\text{spin},L}^2 \rangle_\text{LF}(P^+) = \frac{2}{3} R_A^2, \end{aligned}\end{equation} which indeed coincides with Ref.~\cite{Chen:2024oxx .}
elements.sent-2411-212: Besides, since $\uvec J_{5,\text{LF ^\perp$ in Eq.~(\ref{2DLF-distributions ) assumes exactly the same expression as in Ref.~\cite{Chen:2024oxx , we then naturally obtain the same mean-square transverse LF spin radius~\cite{Chen:2024oxx \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{LF-MS-transverse-radii-2} \langle b_{\text{spin},T}^2 \rangle_\text{LF}(P^+) = \frac{2}{3} R_A^2 + \frac{1}{2M^2} \frac{G_P^Z(0)}{G_A^Z(0) } \end{aligned}\end{equation} for the transverse spin distribution $S_{T,\text{LF (\uvec b_\perp;P^+) \equiv \frac{1 {4 \text{Tr}\left[ \uvec\sigma_\perp \cdot \uvec J_{5,\text{LF ^\perp(\uvec b_\perp;P^+) \right]$.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-213: Similarly to what we found in Eqs.~(\ref{EF-MS-transverse-radii ,\ref{EF-MS-transverse-radii-2 ) in the 2D EF case, these LF results (\ref{LF-MS-transverse-radii , \ref{LF-MS-transverse-radii-2 ) again reconfirm that the second-class current contribution associated with the induced (pseudo-)tensor FF $G_T^Z(Q^2)$, although explicitly included in the full matrix elements (\ref{MatrElem-Axial-Z-LF ) and $G_T^Z(Q^2)$ indeed enters $J_{5,\text{LF ^+$, does not contribute in fact to the mean-square transverse axial and spin radii on the LF.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-214: In this paper, we extended our recent study~\cite{Chen:2024oxx of the relativistic weak-neutral axial-vector four-current distributions inside a general spin-$\frac{1 {2}$ composite system, where the second-class current contribution associated with the weak-neutral induced (pseudo-)tensor form factor is newly but automatically taken into account in terms of the full matrix elements.}}
elements.sent-2411-215: To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time that the full weak-neutral axial-vector four-current distributions of spin-$\frac{1 {2}$ system is systematically studied in terms of relativistic Breit frame, elastic frame and light-front distributions.}
elements.sent-2411-216: We explicitly derived the first exact full tree-level unpolarized differential cross sections of both (anti)neutrino-nucleon (\ref{NC-Z-diffSgm ) and (anti)neutrino-antinucleon (\ref{NC-Z-diffSgm-anti ) elastic scatterings in the lab frame using the full vertex functions including $6$ weak-neutral form factors, individually.}}
elements.sent-2411-217: Relative to previous studies, our formulas of the full tree-level unpolarized differential cross sections are more general and complete, and should be more useful for on-going and future (anti)neutrino-(anti)nucleon elastic scattering experiments.
elements.sent-2411-218: In particular, we explicitly demonstrated that the relativistic weak-neutral 3D axial charge distribution $J^0_{5,B}$ in the Breit frame is in fact related to the induced (pseudo-)tensor form factor $G_T^Z(Q^2)$ rather than the axial form factor $G_A^Z(Q^2)$.
elements.sent-2411-219: Besides, we newly found that $J^0_{5,B}$ (\ref{3DBF-distributions ) is purely imaginary and parity-odd.}
elements.sent-2411-220: This can be understood by the fact that $J^0_{5,B}$ is entirely contributed by the second-class current associated with $G_T^Z(Q^2)$ whose existence signs explicitly the $\mathsf{G}$-parity violation, rather by the ordinary first-class current.
elements.sent-2411-221: Owing to the parity-odd nature of $J^0_{5,B}$, the definition of standard mean-square axial (charge) radius~(\ref{3DBF-MS-axial-radius ) is in fact not well-defined.}
elements.sent-2411-222: We also tested for the first time the Abel transformation of axial charge distributions in the spin-$\frac{1 {2}$ case, through which we explicitly revealed the breakdown of Abel transformation for the connection in physics between 2D light-front and 3D Breit frame axial charge distributions, see Appendix~\ref{Appendix-B .}}
elements.sent-2411-223: On the other hand, the 3D axial-vector current distribution $\uvec J_{5,B}$ is completely free from the second-class current contribution, and is closely related to the physically meaningful 3D spin distribution (\ref{3DBF-spin-density ), from which one can unambiguously define the physically meaningful 3D mean-square spin radius $\langle r_\text{spin ^2 \rangle $ which better characterizes the spatial extension of the weak-neutral content of the system~\cite{Chen:2024oxx .}}}
elements.sent-2411-224: When the system is boosted, the situation gets more complicated since both the Wigner rotation and the mixing of different components under the Lorentz boost will play the roles.
elements.sent-2411-225: We did observe clear frame-dependence of the axial charge distribution $J_{5,\text{EF ^0$ (longitudinal axial-vector current distribution $J_{5,\text{EF ^z$) in the generic EF for a longitudinally (transversely) polarized proton.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-226: The frame-dependence of $J_{5,\text{EF ^0$ and $J_{5,\text{EF ^z$ is solely due to the mixing of temporal and longitudinal components of the axial-vector four-current amplitudes under longitudinal Lorentz boosts (\ref{covariant-Lorentz-Transf ), since they are both free from the Wigner rotation.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-227: On the contrary, the transverse axial-vector current distribution $\uvec J_{5,\text{EF ^\perp$~(\ref{2DEF-distributions ) does not get mixed under longitudinal Lorentz boosts, but it suffers from Wigner rotations (\ref{EF-amplitudes-2 ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-228: We also studied light-front distributions of full weak-neutral axial-vector four-current.
elements.sent-2411-229: We clarified the role played by Melosh rotations, and further proposed and verified the lemma that {\em any light-front amplitudes for well-defined light-front distributions in principle can be explicitly reproduced from the corresponding elastic-frame amplitudes in the proper infinite-momentum-frame limit .}
elements.sent-2411-230: As a reward, we can classify more clearly the distortions appearing in light-front distributions into three key sources.
elements.sent-2411-231: For completeness, we rederived both 3D and 2D transverse mean-square axial and spin radii in different frames.
elements.sent-2411-232: We showed in particular that the second-class current contribution, although explicitly included in our calculations, does not contribution in fact to the mean-square axial and spin radii.
elements.sent-2411-233: To get a more intuitive view of the weak-neutral axial-vector structure of a spin-$\frac{1 {2}$ hadron, we also numerically illustrated our results of the full weak-neutral axial-vector four-current distributions in different frames in the case of a proton, using the proton weak-neutral axial-vector form factors extracted from experimental data, see Appendix~\ref{Appendix-A .}}
elements.sent-2411-234: It should be emphasize that our analytic formulae apply to any spin-$\frac{1 {2}$ hadrons (e.g. $\Lambda^0$, $\Sigma^0$, $\Xi^0$, etc.) and can be easily generalized to higher spin systems, as long as the corresponding weak-neutral axial-vector form factors are available.}

elements.section-2411-008: Parametrization of nucleon weak-neutral axial-vector FFs
elements.sent-2411-236: In the literature~\cite{Horstkotte:1981ne,Ahrens:1986xe,MiniBooNE:2010xqw,MiniBooNE:2013dds,Bernard:2001rs , the nucleon weak-neutral $G_A^Z(Q^2)$ and weak-charged $G_A^W(Q^2)$ axial FFs are usually parametrized in terms of a standard dipole model ans\"atz: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{NC-Z-FF-GA} G_A^L(Q^2) = \frac{G_A^L(0) }{ \left( 1 + \frac{Q^2}{ (M_A^L)^2 } \right)^2 }, \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $L=Z,\, W$ and $M_A^L$ is the corresponding (axial) dipole mass.}
elements.sent-2411-237: We note that the weak-charged nucleon axial FF $G_A^W(Q^2)$ has been extracted from quasielastic (anti)neutrino-nucleon and (anti)neutrino-nuclei scattering data with $M_A^W \approx (1.026 \pm 0.021)\,\text{GeV}$~\cite{Bernard:2001rs .}
elements.sent-2411-238: The weak-charged axial charge (or coupling constant) $G_A^W(0) = (1.2754 \pm 0.0013)$~\cite{ParticleDataGroup:2024cfk is very precisely determined in neutron beta decay reaction $n \to p e^- \bar\nu_e$.}
elements.sent-2411-239: According to Refs.~\cite{Weinberg:1972tu,Horstkotte:1981ne,Ahrens:1986xe,Garvey:1992cg,Garvey:1993sg,Pate:2003rk,Sufian:2018qtw , the weak-neutral axial-vector FFs $G_X^Z(Q^2)$ for $X=A,P,T$ can be related to the corresponding weak-charged axial-vector FFs $G_X^W(Q^2)$ via \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{Relations-NC-CC} G_X^Z(Q^2) = \sum_f g_A^f \, G_X^f(Q^2) \simeq \frac{1}{2}\left[ G_X^{W}(Q^2) - G_X^s(Q^2) + G_X^c(Q^2) - G_X^b(Q^2) + G_X^t(Q^2) \right], \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $G_X^W\simeq G_X^u - G_X^d \equiv G_X^{(u-d)}$, and $G_X^f$ denotes the FF contribution from the $f$-flavor quark with $f=u,d,s,c,b,t$.}
elements.formula-2411-072: \begin{equation}\label{Relations-NC-CC} G_X^Z(Q^2) = \sum_f g_A^f \, G_X^f(Q^2) \simeq \frac{1}{2}\left[ G_X^{W}(Q^2) - G_X^s(Q^2) + G_X^c(Q^2) - G_X^b(Q^2) + G_X^t(Q^2) \right], \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-240: We note that the axial-vector couplings of quarks to the $Z^0$ boson in the Standard Model are given by $g_A^{u,c,t =\frac{1 {2}$ and $g_A^{d,s,b =-\frac{1 {2}$, which explains the overall factor $\frac{1 {2}$ in Eq.~(\ref{Relations-NC-CC ).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-241: In general, the contributions from the heavy-flavor quarks $c$, $b$, $t$ are very small for the nucleon and are therefore usually neglected in practical calculations.
elements.sent-2411-242: In this analysis, we also neglect the contributions from the heavy-flavor quarks for the nucleon.
elements.sent-2411-243: Using directly the extracted experimental data of the nucleon weak-neutral axial FF $G_A^Z(Q^2)$ from Ref.~\cite{Sufian:2018qtw based on recent MiniBooNE measurements~\cite{MiniBooNE:2010xqw,MiniBooNE:2013dds , and performing the standard dipole model (\ref{NC-Z-FF-GA ) fit to the data (denoted as ``Dipole fit"), we find \begin{equation}\label{NC-Z-FF-MA} M_A^Z \approx (1.0500 \pm 0.0107)~\text{GeV},\end{equation} where $G_A^Z(0) = (0.65520 \pm 0.00465)$ is fixed using both world average experimental data of $G_A^W(0) = (1.2754 \pm 0.0013)$ from Particle Data Table~\cite{ParticleDataGroup:2024cfk and the strange quark contribution to the nucleon spin $\Delta s \equiv G_A^s(0) \approx (-0.0350 \pm 0.0092)$ in the continuum limit and physical pion point from lattice QCD~\cite{Liang:2018pis ; see also Refs.~\cite{Pate:2003rk,Pate:2008va,Pate:2024acz .}}}}}}
elements.figure-2411-011: {\includegraphics[angle=0,scale=0.381]{Nucleon_GAZ_v3n.pdf}} {\includegraphics[angle=0,scale=0.385]{Nucleon_GPZ_v3n.pdf}} \caption{Comparison of the nucleon weak-neutral axial $G_A^Z(Q^2)$ (left panel) and induced pseudoscalar $G_P^Z(Q^2)$ (right panel) FFs as a function of $Q^2$ using different methods, where confidence bands of $G_A^Z(Q^2)$ at $95\%$ confidence level are also shown. See texts for more details.} \label{Fig_weakNeutral_GAGP}
elements.sent-2411-244: Alternative to Eq.~(\ref{NC-Z-FF-GA ) with $M_A^Z$ given in Eq.~(\ref{NC-Z-FF-MA ), we can also obtain $G_A^Z(Q^2)$ in terms of $G_A^W(Q^2)$ and $G_A^s(Q^2)$ via Eq.~(\ref{Relations-NC-CC )\footnote{We note that the direct (anti)neutrino-nucleon elastic scattering data~\cite{MiniBooNE:2010xqw,MiniBooNE:2013dds,Sufian:2018qtw of $G_A^Z(Q^2)$ in turn can help us to test whether the relation (\ref{Relations-NC-CC ) between $G_A^Z(Q^2)$ and $G_A^W(Q^2)$ is valid or not.. .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-245: For the strange quark contributions $G_A^s(Q^2)$ and $G_P^s(Q^2)$, $G_X^s(Q^2)$ for $X=A,P$ are also parametrized usually in the literature in terms of the standard dipole model~\cite{Alexandrou:2021wzv \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{NC-Z-FF-s-quark} G_X^s(Q^2) = \frac{ G_X^s(0) }{ \left( 1 + \frac{Q^2}{ (M_X^s)^2 } \right)^2 }, \end{aligned}\end{equation} where the corresponding dipole parameters $G_A^s(0)=-0.044(8)$, $M_A^s=0.992(164)~\text{GeV}$, $G_P^s(0)=-1.325(406)$ and $M_P^s=0.609(89)~\text{GeV}$ can be found in Ref.~\cite{Alexandrou:2021wzv .}}
elements.sent-2411-246: In the left panel of Fig.~\ref{Fig_weakNeutral_GAGP , we show the direct ``Dipole fit" (red dot-dashed curve) result of $G_A^Z(Q^2)$ using the extracted MiniBooNE data~\cite{MiniBooNE:2010xqw,MiniBooNE:2013dds,Sufian:2018qtw (circle markers) via Eq.~(\ref{NC-Z-FF-GA ) and the reconstructed $G_A^Z(Q^2)=[G_A^{W (Q^2) - G_A^s(Q^2)]/2$ labeled by ``Quasi-$\nu N$" (blue solid curve) using Eq.~(\ref{Relations-NC-CC ) in terms of $G_A^W$ (\ref{NC-Z-FF-GA ) extracted from quasi-elastic (anti)neutrino scattering data and $G_A^s$ (\ref{NC-Z-FF-s-quark ), in comparison with $G_A^Z(Q^2)$ from the BNL E734 measurements~\cite{Horstkotte:1981ne,Ahrens:1986xe (orange dotted curve) and the recent ETM lattice QCD calculations~\cite{Alexandrou:2020okk,Alexandrou:2021wzv (green dot-dot-dashed curve), where confidence bands at $95\%$ confidence level are also shown.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-247: Within error bands, all these experimental-data-based results (MiniBooNE, Dipole fit, BNL E734, and Quasi-$\nu N$) are well consistent with each other, which in turn validates the correctness of Eq.~(\ref{Relations-NC-CC ).}
elements.sent-2411-248: In contrast, the ETM lattice result of $G_A^Z(Q^2)$~\cite{Alexandrou:2020okk,Alexandrou:2021wzv shows sizeable deviation from the MiniBooNE, Dipole fit, BNL E734, and Quasi-$\nu N$ results.}
elements.sent-2411-249: To the best of our knowledge, there is currently no direct experimental data of the nucleon weak-neutral induced pseudoscalar FF $G_P^Z(Q^2)$ in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-250: To obtain $G_P^Z(Q^2)$, we need the knowledge of $G_P^W(Q^2)$ and $G_P^s(Q^2)$ according to Eq.~(\ref{Relations-NC-CC ).}
elements.sent-2411-251: The result of $G_P^s(Q^2)$ is given in Eq.~(\ref{NC-Z-FF-s-quark ) where the dipole parameters can be found in Ref.~\cite{Alexandrou:2021wzv .}}
elements.sent-2411-252: The remaining task is to obtain $G_P^W(Q^2)$.
elements.sent-2411-253: In chiral perturbation theory ($\chi$PT), the nucleon weak-charged induced pseudoscalar FF $G_P^W(Q^2)$ from full chiral structure up to next-to-leading-order (NLO) is given by~\cite{Bernard:1994wn,Bernard:2001rs \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{CC-ChPT-FF-GP} G_P^W(Q^2) = g_{\pi^\pm pn} \frac{2(M_p+M_n) F_\pi }{Q^2+M_\pi^2} - 2G_A^W(0) \frac{(M_p+M_n)^2}{(M_A^W)^2 } + \mathcal O(Q^2;M_\pi^2), \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $g_{\pi^\pm pn}$ is the pseudoscalar pion-nucleon coupling constant, $M_p$ ($M_n$) is the proton (neutron) mass, $M_\pi $ is the charged pion mass, $F_\pi=f_{\pi /\sqrt{2 \approx (92.0653 \pm 0.8485)~\text{MeV}$~\cite{ParticleDataGroup:2024cfk is the pion decay constant for the $\pi^+ \to \mu^+ \nu_{\mu}$ reaction, and $M_A^W \approx (1.026 \pm 0.021)\,\text{GeV}$~\cite{Bernard:2001rs .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-254: Based on the recent combined analysis of experimental data using chiral effective field theory for $f_{\pi^\pm pn ^2=0.0769(5)^a(0.9)^b$~\cite{Reinert:2020mcu , we find \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{gPiNN-constant} g_{\pi^\pm pn} = \frac{\sqrt{4\pi} (M_p + M_n) }{ M_\pi } f_{\pi^\pm pn} \approx (13.22613\pm 0.04369), \end{aligned}\end{equation} where uncertainties of $f_{\pi^\pm pn ^2$ from the first ($a$) and second ($b$) errors are added in quadrature.}}}
elements.formula-2411-076: \begin{equation}\label{gPiNN-constant} g_{\pi^\pm pn} = \frac{\sqrt{4\pi} (M_p + M_n) }{ M_\pi } f_{\pi^\pm pn} \approx (13.22613\pm 0.04369), \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-255: Alternatively, we can also obtain $G_P^W(Q^2)$ by assuming the pion-pole dominance (PPD) hypothesis~\cite{Jang:2019vkm,Chen:2022odn , which is based on the low-energy QCD relations---the partially conserved axial-vector current (PCAC) relation and the Goldberger-Treiman relation~\cite{Goldberger:1958tr , namely \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{CC-PPD-FF-GP} G_P^W(Q^2) = \frac{ (M_p+M_n)^2 }{ M_{\pi}^2 + Q^2 } G_A^W(Q^2), \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $G_A^W(Q^2)$ is extracted from quasielastic (anti)neutrino scattering data~\cite{Bernard:2001rs via Eq.~(\ref{NC-Z-FF-GA ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-256: We can thus construct $G_P^Z(Q^2) = [G_P^W(Q^2) - G_P^s(Q^2) ]/2$ with known $G_P^s(Q^2)$ (\ref{NC-Z-FF-s-quark ) by using $G_P^W(Q^2)$ either from Eq.~(\ref{CC-ChPT-FF-GP ) which is labeled by ``NLO $\chi$PT", or from Eq.~(\ref{CC-PPD-FF-GP ) which is labeled by ``PPD + Quasi-$\nu N$".}}}
elements.sent-2411-257: The PPD hypothesis also inspires us to reconstruct $G_P^Z(Q^2)$ by using directly the ``Dipole fit" $G_A^Z(Q^2)$ from Eqs.~(\ref{NC-Z-FF-GA ) and (\ref{NC-Z-FF-MA ), namely \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{NC-PPD-FF-GP} G_P^Z(Q^2) = \frac{ 4M^2 }{ M_{\pi}^2 + Q^2 } G_A^Z(Q^2), \end{aligned}\end{equation} which is labeled by ``PPD + Dipole fit".}}
elements.sent-2411-258: In the right panel of Fig.~\ref{Fig_weakNeutral_GAGP , we present our results of $G_P^Z(Q^2)$ by using different methods: ``PPD + Dipole fit" (red dot-dashed curve), ``PPD + Quasi-$\nu N$" (blue solid curve), and ``NLO $\chi$PT", in comparison with the reconstructed $G_P^Z(Q^2)=[G_P^W(Q^2)-G_P^s(Q^2)]/2$ with $G_P^s(Q^2)$ (\ref{NC-Z-FF-s-quark ) by using experimental data of $G_P^W(Q^2)$ from the ordinary $\mu$-capture measurements~\cite{Bardin:1981cq,MuCap:2012lei at\footnote{More rigorously, $Q^2 = \left[ \frac{(M_\mu + M_p)^2 - M_n^2 {M_\mu (M_\mu+M_p) - 1 \right] M_\mu^2 = \left[ \frac{ M_p (M_\mu + M_p)-M_n^2 {M_\mu(M_\mu + M_p ) \right] M_\mu^2 \approx 0.88~M_\mu^2$, where $M_\mu$ is the muon mass.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-259: Besides, we also show the results of $G_P^Z(Q^2) = [G_P^{(u-d) -G_P^s]/2$ from the recent ETM lattice QCD calculations~\cite{Alexandrou:2020okk,Alexandrou:2021wzv labeled by ``ETM lattice" (green dot-dot-dashed curve).}}
elements.sent-2411-260: We find that all these results of $G_P^Z$ are well consistent with each other, which also indicates the validity of Eq.~(\ref{Relations-NC-CC ).}
elements.sent-2411-261: Owing to the intensive overlaps of these results, the confidence bands for $G_P^Z(Q^2)$ at $95\%$ confidence level are not shown.
elements.sent-2411-262: For the nucleon weak-neutral induced (pseudo-)tensor FF $G_T^Z$, there is currently no direct experimental data at all.
elements.sent-2411-263: To some extent, this is also the reason why the second-class current contribution of the nucleon associated with the induced (pseudo-)tensor FF $G_T^Z(Q^2)$ are scarcely discussed and calculated in the literature~\cite{Meissner:1986xg,Meissner:1986js,Bernard:1994wn,Silva:2005fa,Schindler:2006jq,Aliev:2007qu,Sharma:2009hg,Eichmann:2011pv,Liu:2014owa,Dahiya:2014jfa,Ramalho:2015jem,Anikin:2016teg,Mamedov:2016ype,Hashamipour:2019pgy,Mondal:2019jdg,Zhang:2019iyx,Jun:2020lfx,Chen:2020wuq,Chen:2021guo,Ahmady:2021qed,Sauerwein:2021jxb,Xu:2021wwj,Atayev:2022omk,Chen:2022odn,Cheng:2022jxe,Liu:2022ekr,Ramalho:2024tdi .}
elements.sent-2411-264: According to the Fig.~7 of Ref.~\cite{Day:2012gb , one can assume that $G_T^W(Q^2)$ can by roughly approximated by $G_T^W(Q^2)\equiv \kappa_T G_A^W(Q^2)$, where the factor $\kappa_T \approx 0.1$ is roughly the mean value of the ratio $G_T^W(0)/G_A^W(0)$ in the Fig.~7 of Ref.~\cite{Day:2012gb with $G_A^W = F_A$ and $G_T^W = 2F_A^{3}$, and $G_A^W(Q^2)$ is given in Eq.~(\ref{NC-Z-FF-GA ) with $M_A^W \approx (1.026 \pm 0.021)\,\text{GeV}$~\cite{Bernard:2001rs .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-265: As a reward, Ref.~\cite{Day:2012gb thus inspires us to propose the following ans\"atz for the nucleon weak-neutral induced (pseudo-)tensor FF $G_T^Z(Q^2)$: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{NC-Z-FF-GT} G_T^W(Q^2) = \kappa_T G_A^Z(Q^2), \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $G_A^Z(Q^2)$ is the direct dipole model (\ref{NC-Z-FF-GA ) fit to the elastic (anti)neutrino-nucleon scattering data~\cite{MiniBooNE:2010xqw,MiniBooNE:2013dds,Sufian:2018qtw .}}}
elements.sent-2411-266: We should emphasize that the reason why we relate $G_T^Z(Q^2)$ with $G_A^Z(Q^2)$ via the ans\"atz (\ref{NC-Z-FF-GT ) is simply because the assumption proposed in Ref.~\cite{Day:2012gb , which is the only reference that we have ever found with experimentally reasonable and useful relation for $G_T^W(Q^2)$ [and thus for $G_T^Z(Q^2)$].}}
elements.sent-2411-267: In the real word, $G_T^Z(Q^2)$ is most probably to be quite different from $G_A^Z(Q^2)$ instead of the simple and naive scaling ans\"atz (\ref{NC-Z-FF-GT ), since $G_T^W(Q^2)$ is strongly constrained by many symmetries and conservation laws while $G_A^W(Q^2)$ is not.}
elements.sent-2411-268: This provides also a key motivation for future experimental measurements of $G_T^Z(Q^2)$ and $G_T^W(Q^2)$ for the nucleon in (anti)neutrino-nucleon elastic and quasielastic scatterings, respectively, e.g. using the full tree-level unpolarized differential cross sections (\ref{NC-Z-diffSgm ).}
elements.sent-2411-269: For numerical calculations and demonstrations of the nucleon weak-neutral axial-vector four-current distributions in Sec.~\ref{sec:Breit frame distributions to Sec.~\ref{sec:Light-front distributions , we declare that we employ only nucleon weak-neutral axial-vector FFs $G_A^Z(Q^2)$ from Eq.~(\ref{NC-Z-FF-GA ) via the direct dipole-model fit (``Dipole fit") to the elastic (anti)neutrino-nucleon scattering data~\cite{MiniBooNE:2010xqw,MiniBooNE:2013dds,Sufian:2018qtw , $G_P^Z(Q^2)$ from Eq.~(\ref{NC-PPD-FF-GP ) using the ``Dipole fit" $G_A^Z(Q^2)$ and the PPD hypothesis (\ref{NC-PPD-FF-GP ), and $G_T^Z(Q^2)$ from Eq.~(\ref{NC-Z-FF-GT ) using the ``Dipole fit" $G_A^Z(Q^2)$ and $\kappa_T \approx 0.1$.}}}}}}}
elements.section-2411-009: Breakdown of Abel transformation for axial charge distributions
elements.sent-2411-270: The Abel and its inverse transforms, named after Niels~H.~Abel for integral transforms in mathematics, have been visited recently in several works~\cite{Moiseeva:2008qd,Panteleeva:2021iip,Kim:2021jjf,Kim:2021kum,Kim:2022bia,Kim:2022syw,Choudhary:2022den in the case of charge and energy-momentum tensor spatial distributions with the goal of connecting 2D LF spatial distributions with the corresponding 3D ones, where 2D LF spatial distributions are regarded as the 2D Abel images of the corresponds 3D spatial distributions.}
elements.sent-2411-271: We notice that some discussions and debates have already been triggered in Refs.~\cite{Freese:2021mzg,Panteleeva:2021iip .}
elements.sent-2411-272: In this appendix, we will explicitly show the breakdown of Abel and its inverse transforms for the connection between 2D LF and 3D BF axial charge distributions in the spin-$\frac{1 {2}$ case.}
elements.sent-2411-273: According to textbooks, the standard definitions of the Abel and its inverse transforms are given by~\cite{Bracewell2000bok \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{AbelTransform-standard} \mathscr A\left[g \right] (b) \equiv \mathcal{G}(b) =2\int_{b}^{\infty}\ud r\frac{r}{\sqrt{r^2-b^2}}g(r), g(r) = -\frac{1}{\pi}\int_{r}^{\infty}\mathrm{d}b \frac{1}{\sqrt{b^2-r^2}}\frac{\ud \mathcal{G}(b) }{\ud b}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\mathscr A\left[g \right] (b)\equiv \mathcal{G (b)$ is called the 2D Abel image of the 3D spatial function $g(r)$~\cite{Freese:2021mzg .}}}
elements.formula-2411-080: \begin{equation}\label{AbelTransform-standard} \mathscr A\left[g \right] (b) \equiv G(b) =2\int_{b}^{\infty}\ud r\frac{r}{\sqrt{r^2-b^2}}g(r), g(r) = -\frac{1}{\pi}\int_{r}^{\infty}db \frac{1}{\sqrt{b^2-r^2}}\frac{\ud G(b) }{\ud b}, \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-274: It is thus not difficult to obtain the following generic relation for the $n$th order Mellin moment of the Abel image $\mathcal{G (b)$ in connecting with the corresponding 3D spatial function $g(r)$: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{Mellin-moments} \int_0^{\infty}\ud b\, b^{n-1}\, \mathcal{G}(b) = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}\, \Gamma\left(\frac{n}{2} \right)}{\Gamma\left(\frac{n+1}{2} \right) } \int_0^{\infty} \ud r\, r^{n}g(r),\qquad n \in \mathbb N^+ =\{1,2,3,\cdots\}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} which is believed to be valid as long as $g(r)$ decreases faster than any order of $r^{n}$~\cite{Kim:2021kum .}}
elements.formula-2411-081: \begin{equation}\label{Mellin-moments} \int_0^{\infty}\ud b\, b^{n-1}\, G(b) = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}\, \Gamma\left(\frac{n}{2} \right)}{\Gamma\left(\frac{n+1}{2} \right) } \int_0^{\infty} \ud r\, r^{n}g(r),\qquad n \in \mathbb N^+ =\{1,2,3,\cdots\}, \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-275: Using the dipole model ans\"atz (\ref{NC-Z-FF-GA ) for the axial FF $G_A^Z(Q^2)$, one can easily obtain the following analytic expression of the LF axial charge distribution from Eq.~(\ref{2DLF-distributions ) for a longitudinally polarized spin-$\frac{1 {2}$ target (with $b=|\uvec b_\perp|$): \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{2DLF-Jp-Dipole-spinL} J_{5,\text{LF}}^{+}(\uvec b_\perp;P^+) = (\sigma_z)_{\lambda' \lambda } G_A^Z(0) \frac{b (M_A^{Z})^3}{4\pi}\, K_1(b M_A^{Z}) \equiv J_{5,\text{LF}}^{+}(b), \end{aligned}\end{equation} which is axially symmetric and can be regarded as the 2D Abel image of a 3D spatial distribution $J^{0 _{5,\text{naive (r)$.}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-082: \begin{equation}\label{2DLF-Jp-Dipole-spinL} J_{5,LF}^{+}(\uvec b_\perp;P^+) = (\sigma_z)_{\lambda' \lambda } G_A^Z(0) \frac{b (M_A^{Z})^3}{4\pi}\, K_1(b M_A^{Z}) \equiv J_{5,LF}^{+}(b), \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-276: Applying the inverse Abel transform (\ref{AbelTransform-standard ) to $J_{5,\text{LF ^{+}$(b), we find \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{3DAbel-J0-spinL} J^{0}_{5,\text{naive}}(r) = (\sigma_z)_{\lambda' \lambda } G_A^Z(0)\frac{ (M_A^{Z})^3}{8\pi}\, e^{-M_A^{Z} r}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} One can first check that the axial charge normalization condition \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} (\sigma_z)_{\lambda' \lambda } G_A^Z(0) = \int\ud^3 r \, J^{0}_{5,\text{naive}}(r) =\int \ud^2 b_\perp \, J_{5,\text{LF}}^{+}(b) \end{aligned}\end{equation} seems to be automatically guaranteed.}}}
elements.sent-2411-277: Besides, one can also check that $J_{5,\text{LF ^{+ (b)$ and $J^{0 _{5,\text{naive (r)$ indeed satisfy the generic relation (\ref{Mellin-moments ) for the corresponding Mellin moment at any order.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-278: In particular, we find that the mean-square axial (charge) radius of $J^{0 _{5,\text{naive (r)$ is given by \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{Abel-Axial-J0-MSR} \langle r_A^2 \rangle_{\text{naive}}^\text{Abel} = \frac{\int \ud^3r\, r^2 J^{0}_{5,\text{naive}}(r) }{ \int \ud^3r\, J^{0}_{5,\text{naive}}(r) } = \frac{12}{(M_A^{Z})^2} \approx (0.6510~\text{fm})^2 = - \frac{6}{G_A^Z(0)} \frac{\ud G_A^Z(Q^2)}{\ud Q^2} \bigg|_{Q^2=0} = R_A^2, \end{aligned}\end{equation} which exactly coincides with the identification of $R_A^2$ via Eq.~(\ref{naive-3DMS-axial-radius ) widely employed in the literature~\cite{Meissner:1986xg,Meissner:1986js,Bernard:1992ys,A1:1999kwj,Meyer:2016oeg,Hill:2017wgb,MINERvA:2023avz,Petti:2023abz,Kaiser:2024vbc .}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-085: \begin{equation}\label{Abel-Axial-J0-MSR} \langle r_A^2 \rangle_{naive}^Abel = \frac{\int \ud^3r\, r^2 J^{0}_{5,naive}(r) }{ \int \ud^3r\, J^{0}_{5,naive}(r) } = \frac{12}{(M_A^{Z})^2} \approx (0.6510~fm)^2 = - \frac{6}{G_A^Z(0)} \frac{\ud G_A^Z(Q^2)}{\ud Q^2} \bigg|_{Q^2=0} = R_A^2, \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-279: In contrast to $J^{0 _{5,\text{naive }$ (\ref{3DAbel-J0-spinL ), the genuine 3D axial charge distribution (\ref{3DBF-distributions ) for a longitudinally polarized target is in fact given by \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{3Dgenuine-J0-spinZ} J_{5,B}^0(\uvec r) = (\sigma_z)_{s's}\int\frac{\ud^3\Delta}{(2\pi)^3}\, e^{-i\uvec\Delta \cdot \uvec r}\, \frac{\Delta_z }{2M} G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta^2), \end{aligned}\end{equation} which is actually related to the induced (pseudo-)tensor FF $G_T^Z(Q^2)$ rather than the axial FF $G_A^Z(Q^2)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-280: This explicitly demonstrates that even though we neglect the polarization difference, the naive 3D distribution $J^{0,(L) _{5,\text{naive (r)$ does not assume clear physical meaning for quantifying the genuine 3D spatial distribution of weak-neutral axial charges in the BF for a longitudinally polarized spin-$\frac{1 {2}$ target.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-281: This explicitly reveals for the first time the breakdown of Abel and its inverse transforms for the connection in physics between 2D LF and 3D BF axial charge distributions in the spin-$\frac{1 {2}$ case.}
elements.sent-2411-282: It should be noted that the discussions on the breakdown of Abel transformations for targets with spin $j\geq 1$ have recently been presented in Ref.~\cite{Freese:2021mzg .}
elements.sent-2411-283: Axial-vector FFs are Lorentz-invariant functions that describe how the hadron reacts with the incoming (anti)neutrino in a scattering reaction, encoding therefore very clean internal axial charge and spin information of the hadron in the weak sector since (anti)neutrinos participate only in weak interactions.
elements.sent-2411-284: On the theory side, tremendous progress has been made in the last few years from first principle lattice QCD calculations of these axial-vector FFs~\cite{Bhattacharya:2016zcn,Liang:2016fgy,Green:2017keo,Gupta:2017dwj,Yao:2017fym,Hasan:2017wwt,Ishikawa:2018rew,Shintani:2018ozy,Hasan:2019noy,Jang:2019vkm,RQCD:2019jai,Lin:2020rxa,Alexandrou:2020okk,Park:2021ypf,Alexandrou:2021wzv,Ishikawa:2021eut,Meyer:2021vfq,Schulz:2021kwz,Alexandrou:2021jok,Lin:2021brq,Alexandrou:2022yrr,Djukanovic:2022wru,Lin:2022nnj,Koponen:2022gfe,Jang:2023zts,Alexandrou:2023qbg ; theoretical evaluations of these axial-vector FFs and cross sections based on chiral perturbation theory and various models are still vividly developing~\cite{Meissner:1986xg,Meissner:1986js,Bernard:1994wn,Ohlsson:1998bk,Barquilla-Cano:2002btm,Silva:2005fa,Schindler:2006jq,Aliev:2007qu,Sharma:2009hg,Eichmann:2011pv,Liu:2014owa,Dahiya:2014jfa,Ramalho:2015jem,Anikin:2016teg,Mamedov:2016ype,Hashamipour:2019pgy,Mondal:2019jdg,Zhang:2019iyx,Jun:2020lfx,Chen:2020wuq,Chen:2021guo,Ahmady:2021qed,Sauerwein:2021jxb,Xu:2021wwj,Atayev:2022omk,Chen:2022odn,Cheng:2022jxe,Liu:2022ekr,Irani:2023lol,Ramalho:2024tdi,Tomalak:2022xup,Sobczyk:2024ecl,SajjadAthar:2024etq .}}
elements.sent-2411-285: For ongoing and future experimental measurements and data analyses of elastic (anti)neutrino-(anti)nucleon scatterings~\cite{Horstkotte:1981ne,Ahrens:1986xe,MiniBooNE:2010xqw,MiniBooNE:2013dds , we also derive the first exact full tree-level unpolarized differential cross sections of both (anti)neutrino-nucleon and (anti)neutrino-antinucleon elastic scatterings in the lab frame using the full vertex functions including $6$ FFs individually.}
elements.sent-2411-286: For completeness, we also provide details for the parametrization of nucleon weak-neutral axial-vector form factors in Appendix~\ref{Appendix-A , and further discussions on the breakdown of Abel transformation of axial charge distributions in Appendix~\ref{Appendix-B .}}
elements.sent-2411-287: For better legibility, we have omitted the hadron index ``$\text{B}$" attached to momentum-eigenstates, and the explicit $Q^2$-dependence of these weak-neutral axial-vector FFs $G_A^Z(Q^2)$, $G_P^Z(Q^2)$ and $G_T^Z(Q^2)$.
elements.sent-2411-288: Since the explicit (diagonal) matrix forms of generators $T_a$ associated with the operator $\hat j_{5,a ^{\mu (x)\equiv \hat{\bar \psi (x) \gamma^\mu \gamma^5 T_a \hat\psi(x)$, see e.g. Ref.~\cite{Bernard:2001rs , for a given flavor-space fundamental representation group $SU(n)_f$ ($n \geq 2$ and $n \in \mathbb Z$) of a given (anti)neutrino-hadron elastic scattering reaction are known, it is convenient to work in the $U(1)_f$ representation without loss of generality.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-289: Using Weinberg's classification, we can thus identify $G_A^Z$ and $G_P^Z$ in Eq.~(\ref{MatrElem-Axial-Z ) as the FFs associated with the first-class currents, while identify $G_T^Z$ as the FF associated with the second-class current.}
elements.sent-2411-290: Likewise Eq.~(\ref{MatrElem-Axial-Z ), we have also omitted the explicit $Q^2$-dependence for the weak-neutral vector FFs $G_E^Z$, $G_M^Z$ and $G_S^Z$, where $G_{E ^Z$ and $G_M^Z$ are the weak-neutral electromagnetic FFs associated with the first-class currents, and $G_S^Z$ is the weak-neutral induced scalar FF associated with the second-class current.}}
elements.sent-2411-291: If one imposes the hypothesis of conserved weak-neutral vector four-current (or the Ward identities of weak-neutral vector four-current quantum amplitudes) that also follows naturally from the Standard Model, it is evident that $G_S^Z(Q^2)=0$~\cite{Holstein:1984ga .}
elements.sent-2411-292: For neutrino-nucleon elastic scattering reaction (with $\ell=e,\mu,\tau$) \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \nu_\ell(k,r) + N(p,s) \to \nu_\ell(k',r') + N(p',s'), \end{aligned}\end{equation} the scattering amplitude $i\mathcal M$ in the Feynman gauge is given by \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} i\mathcal M = (-i\, \xi_C) \left( \frac{M_Z^2}{M_Z^2+Q^2} \right) \frac{G_F}{ \sqrt{2} } L_\mu J^\mu_\text{weak}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} with \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} L_\mu \equiv \bar u(k',r')\gamma_\mu (1-\gamma^5) u(k,r), J^\mu_\text{weak} \equiv \bar u(p',s') \Gamma^\mu_{V-A} u(p,s), \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\xi_C=1$ (for weak-neutral currents), $M_Z\approx 91.1880(20)~\text{GeV}$ is the $Z^0$ boson mass~\cite{ParticleDataGroup:2024cfk , $G_F$ is the Fermi coupling constant, $\Gamma^\mu_{V-A}$ is the full vertex function given in Eq.~(\ref{MatrElem-VmA-Z ), and we have used the fact that \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \frac{ G_F }{ \sqrt{2} } = \frac{g^2}{8M_Z^2 \cos^2 \theta_W}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} with $g$ the universal weak coupling constant and $\theta_W$ the Weinberg angle.}}
elements.formula-2411-088: \begin{equation} L_\mu \equiv \bar u(k',r')\gamma_\mu (1-\gamma^5) u(k,r), J^\mu_weak \equiv \bar u(p',s') \Gamma^\mu_{V-A} u(p,s), \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-293: Last but not least, we did realize during the derivations of the full tree-level unpolarized differential cross sections (\ref{NC-Z-diffSgm , \ref{NC-Z-diffSgm-anti ) that polarized differential cross sections of polarized (anti)neutrino-(anti)nucleon elastic scatterings will provide us more rich and complete information about nucleon weak-neutral vector and axial-vector FFs in the weak sector, where FFs $G_P^Z$ (or $\bar G_P^Z$) and $G_S^Z$ (or $\bar G_S^Z$) may explicitly appear in the polarized differential cross sections\footnote{We note that two virtual $Z^0$ bosons exchange (or multiple virtual $Z^0$ bosons exchange) and radiative corrections at one-loop order (or even higher loops) may also provide us the possibilities that nucleon FFs $G_P^Z$ (or $\bar G_P^Z$) and $G_S^Z$ (or $\bar G_S^Z$) may explicitly appear in the unpolarized differential cross sections.}}}
elements.sent-2411-294: In Fig.~\ref{Fig_3DBFNC_J0 , we illustrate the BF axial charge distributions $iJ_{5,B ^0(\uvec r) = -\text{Im}\left[ J_{5,B ^0(\uvec r) \right] $ and $4\pi r^2 \cdot iJ_{5,B ^0(\uvec r) $ with $r=|\uvec r|$ along the $z$-axis inside a longitudinally polarized proton, using proton's weak-neutral induced (pseudo-)tensor FF $G_T^Z(Q^2)$ given in Appendix~\ref{Appendix-A .}}}}}
elements.figure-2411-012: {\includegraphics[angle=0,scale=0.60]{Nucleon_3DBFNCJ0_v3n.pdf}} \caption{BF weak-neutral axial charge distributions $iJ_{5,B}^0(\uvec r) = -\text{Im}[ J_{5,B}^0(\uvec r) ] $ (upper panel) and $4\pi r^2 \cdot iJ_{5,B}^0(\uvec r) $ (lower panel) along the $z$-axis inside a longitudinally polarized (i.e. $\hvec s \equiv \uvec\sigma_{s's} = \uvec e_z$) proton, using proton's weak-neutral induced (pseudo-)tensor FF $G_T^Z(Q^2)$ given in Appendix~\ref{Appendix-A}.} \label{Fig_3DBFNC_J0}
elements.sent-2411-295: Using the QCD equation of motion~\cite{Leader:2013jra , one can show that~\cite{Lorce:2017wkb,Chen:2024oxx \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{3DBF-spin-density} \uvec S_B(\uvec r) = \frac{1}{2}\uvec J_{5,B}(\uvec r) \end{aligned}\end{equation} is the physically meaningful 3D spin distribution in the BF, characterizing how the spin is distributed in the weak neutral sector.}}
elements.sent-2411-296: Using proton's weak-neutral axial-vector FFs $G_A^Z(Q^2)$ and $G_P^Z(Q^2)$ given in Appendix~\ref{Appendix-A , we find that the weak-neutral mean-square spin radius of the proton is given by~\cite{Chen:2024oxx \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{3DBF-mean-square-radii} \langle r_\text{spin}^2 \rangle^p = \frac{1}{G_A^Z(0)}\left[ -\uvec\nabla_{\uvec\Delta}^2 G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta^2) \right] \Big|_{\uvec\Delta = \uvec 0} + \frac{1}{4M^2}\left[ 1 + \frac{2 G_P^Z(0) }{ G_A^Z(0) } \right] \equiv r_S^2 \approx (2.1054~\text{fm})^2, \end{aligned}\end{equation} where the last term $\left( \frac{1 {2M^2 \frac{G_P^Z(0) { G_A^Z(0) \right)$ plays the dominant role since the ratio $G_P^Z(0)/G_A^Z(0) = (2M_p/M_\pi)^2 \approx 180.772$ is very large; see Appendix~\ref{Appendix-A for more details of $G_A^Z(Q^2)$ and $G_P^Z(Q^2)$.}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-298: In the case when $G_T^Z(Q^2)=0$ by using $\mathsf G$-parity invariance of QCD or exact isospin symmetry, the 3D axial density $J_{5,B ^0(\uvec r)$ vanishes identically for a generic spin-$\frac{1 {2}$ target and thus the axial (charge) radius does not exist~\cite{Chen:2024oxx , contrary to what is usually stated in the literature~\cite{Meissner:1986xg,Meissner:1986js,Bernard:1992ys,A1:1999kwj,Meyer:2016oeg,Hill:2017wgb,MINERvA:2023avz,Petti:2023abz,Kaiser:2024vbc via \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{naive-3DMS-axial-radius} R_A^2 \equiv -\frac{6}{G_A^Z(0) }\frac{\ud G_A^Z(Q^2) }{\ud Q^2} \bigg|_{Q^2=0} = \frac{1}{G_A^Z(0)}\left[ -\uvec\nabla_{\uvec\Delta}^2 G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta^2) \right] \Big|_{\uvec\Delta = \uvec 0}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} In conclusion, in either $G_T^Z(Q^2) = 0$ or $G_T^Z(Q^2) \neq 0$ cases, the genuine 3D mean-square axial (charge) radius $\langle r_A^2 \rangle $ is not defined via the slope of the axial FF $G_A^Z(Q^2)$ at $Q^2=0$, since the 3D axial charge distribution itself is related to $G_T^Z(Q^2)$ rather than $G_A^Z(Q^2)$.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-310: Applying the general definition (\ref{LF-def ) to the axial-vector four-current operator and inserting the EF amplitudes (\ref{LF-amplitudes ), we obtain the following LF weak-neutral axial-vector four-current distributions~\cite{Chen:2024oxx,Diehl:2005jf : \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{2DLF-distributions} J_{5,\text{LF}}^+(\uvec b_\perp;P^+) = \int \frac{\ud^2 \Delta_\perp}{(2\pi)^2}\, e^{-i\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec b_\perp }\, \left[ (\sigma_z)_{\lambda' \lambda} G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \frac{ (\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec\sigma_\perp)_{\lambda' \lambda} }{2M} G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \right] , J_{5,\text{LF}}^-(\uvec b_\perp;P^+) = \int \frac{\ud^2 \Delta_\perp}{(2\pi)^2}\, e^{-i\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec b_\perp }\, \frac{P^-}{P^+} \left[ -(\sigma_z)_{\lambda' \lambda} G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) + \frac{ (\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec\sigma_\perp)_{\lambda' \lambda} }{2M} G_T^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \right] , \uvec J_{5,\text{LF}}^\perp(\uvec b_\perp;P^+) = \int\frac{\ud^2\Delta_\perp }{(2\pi)^2 }\, e^{-i\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec b_\perp }\, \frac{M}{P^+} \bigg\{ \left[ (\uvec\sigma_\perp)_{\lambda' \lambda} + \frac{(\uvec e_z \times i\uvec\Delta_\perp)_\perp }{2M} \delta_{ \lambda' \lambda } \right]G_A^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \qquad - \frac{\uvec\Delta_\perp \left(\uvec\Delta_\perp \cdot \uvec\sigma_\perp \right)_{\lambda'\lambda} }{ 4M^2 } G_P^Z(\uvec\Delta_\perp^2) \bigg\}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} In the case $G_T^Z(\uvec \Delta_\perp^2)=0$, we do explicitly reproduce the LF axial-vector four-current distributions given in Ref.~\cite{Chen:2024oxx .}}}}

elements.sent-2411-326: According to Refs.~\cite{Weinberg:1972tu,Horstkotte:1981ne,Ahrens:1986xe,Garvey:1992cg,Garvey:1993sg,Pate:2003rk,Sufian:2018qtw , the weak-neutral axial-vector FFs $G_X^Z(Q^2)$ for $X=A,P,T$ can be related to the corresponding weak-charged ones $G_X^W(Q^2)$ via \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{Relations-NC-CC} G_X^Z(Q^2) = \sum_f g_A^f \, G_X^f(Q^2) \simeq \frac{1}{2}\left[ G_X^{W}(Q^2) - G_X^s(Q^2) + G_X^c(Q^2) - G_X^b(Q^2) + G_X^t(Q^2) \right], \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $G_X^W\simeq G_X^u - G_X^d \equiv G_X^{(u-d)}$, and $G_X^f$ denotes the FF contribution from the $f$-flavor quark with $f=u,d,s,c,b,t$.}
elements.sent-2411-327: It should be noted that $G_A^Z(t=-Q^2)$ and $G_P^Z(t=-Q^2)$ can also be accessed via measurements of generalized parton distributions (GPDs) $\tilde H_f(x,\xi,t)$ and $\tilde E_f(x,\xi,t)$ using facilities at JLab, DESY, CERN, BNL, etc. through the first-moment sum rules~\cite{Ji:1996ek,Diehl:2003ny,JeffersonLabHallA:2022pnx \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{relation_to_GPDs} \begin{bmatrix} G_A^Z(t) G_P^Z(t) \end{bmatrix} = \sum_f g_A^f \int_{-1}^{1} \ud x \begin{bmatrix} \tilde H_f(x,\xi,t) \tilde E_f(x,\xi,t) \end{bmatrix}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} In general, the contributions from the heavy-flavor quarks (i.e. $c$, $b$, $t$) are very small for the nucleon and are therefore usually neglected in practical calculations.}

elements.sent-2411-329: Using directly the extracted experimental data of the nucleon weak-neutral axial FF $G_A^Z(Q^2)$ from Ref.~\cite{Sufian:2018qtw based on recent MiniBooNE measurements~\cite{MiniBooNE:2010xqw,MiniBooNE:2013dds , and performing the standard dipole model (\ref{NC-Z-FF-GA ) fit to the data (denoted as ``Dipole fit"), we find \begin{equation}\label{NC-Z-FF-MA} M_A^Z \approx (1.0500 \pm 0.0107)~\text{GeV},\end{equation} where $G_A^Z(0) = (0.65520 \pm 0.00465)$ is fixed using both world average experimental data of $G_A^W(0) = (1.2754 \pm 0.0013)$ from Particle Data Table~\cite{ParticleDataGroup:2024cfk and the strange quark contribution to the nucleon spin $\Delta s \equiv G_A^s(0) \approx (-0.0350 \pm 0.0092)$ in the continuum limit and physical pion point from lattice QCD~\cite{Liang:2018pis ; see also Refs.~\cite{Pate:2003rk,Pate:2008va,Pate:2024acz for recent analyses of $G_A^s(0)$.}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-344: As an extension of our recent work~\cite{Chen:2024oxx , we study in this work in detail the relativistic full weak-neutral axial-vector four-current distributions inside a general spin-$\tfrac{1 {2}$ hadron (e.g., the proton), where the second-class current contribution associated with the induced pseudotensor FF is newly taken into account.}}
elements.sent-2411-345: We explicitly demonstrate that the relativistic 3D weak-neutral axial charge distribution, being parity-odd in the BF, is in fact related to the weak-neutral induced pseudotensor FF $G_T^Z(Q^2)$ rather than the axial FF $G_A^Z(Q^2)$.

elements.sent-2411-347: We also study the full weak-neutral axial-vector four-current distributions for a moving hadron focusing on their frame-dependence, and compare them with their LF counterparts.
elements.sent-2411-348: In particular, we explicitly reproduce the LF axial-vector four-current amplitudes via the proper IMF limit of the corresponding elastic frame (EF) amplitudes, which together with our recent works~\cite{Chen:2022smg,Chen:2023dxp,Chen:2024oxx,Lorce:2024ipy,Lorce:2024ewq inspires us to propose the following conjecture: {\em Any light-front amplitudes for well-defined light-front distributions in principle can be explicitly reproduced from the corresponding elastic frame amplitudes in the proper infinite-momentum frame limit .}}
elements.sent-2411-349: As a reward, we can understand more easily and intuitively the origins of distortions appearing in LF distributions (relative to the BF ones).
